title: Network security control processes defined and understood
description: Processes and mechanisms for implementing network security controls are defined and understood.
guidance: Document and maintain all firewall and router configuration standards and review them at least every six months.
implementationGuide: Pull a full export of every firewall and router configuration in scope and map each device to the network boundary it protects — do this before writing a single line of policy. Use Tufin Orchestration Suite or AlgoSec Firewall Analyzer to auto-generate a topology diagram and flag rules with no documented business justification. The configuration standard document must specify allowed protocols, required rule review cadence, and a change-approval workflow signed by a network owner. 'Done' means every in-scope device has a corresponding standard, the standard has been approved in the last 12 months, and Tufin shows zero rules without a justification ticket. A common QSA catch is a standard that exists on paper but hasn't been updated since the last audit cycle — version control with dated approvals closes this gap. SAQ A merchants with fully outsourced card processing still need to document the network boundary between their environment and the service provider; SAQ D and ROC assessments require evidence of the full review cycle.
exampleEvidence: Tufin Orchestration Suite topology report dated within 90 days, showing all CDE-adjacent firewalls and routers with rule counts and last-reviewed timestamps
Network security control configuration standard document, version-controlled, with approval signatures from the CISO and network owner dated within 12 months
Change-management ticket log from ServiceNow or Jira showing every firewall rule change in the last 6 months, each linked to a business justification and approver
AlgoSec Firewall Analyzer compliance report showing zero rules flagged as 'undocumented' or 'no justification' across all CDE-boundary devices
Meeting minutes or email thread from the most recent semi-annual rule-base review, listing attendees, rules examined, and decisions made
Network diagram (Visio or Lucidchart export) annotated with CDE boundary, trust zones, and device ownership, approved by the network architect

title: Network security controls implemented
description: Network security controls (NSCs) are configured and maintained.
guidance: Implement deny-all, permit-by-exception firewall rules between all networks and review rule sets every six months.
implementationGuide: Begin by deploying and enforcing a deny-all-then-permit baseline on every firewall and router at the CDE boundary — this is the single configuration change that most directly reduces scope risk. Use Palo Alto NGFW or Cisco Firepower with App-ID enabled to enforce application-layer controls rather than relying on port-based rules. Every permitted connection must have a corresponding entry in a network access matrix, reviewed quarterly and signed by the responsible business owner. 'Done' means a live Palo Alto Security Policy export shows no rules using 'any' in the service or application column for traffic entering the CDE, and the access matrix matches the ruleset exactly. QSAs routinely find rules added for one-off troubleshooting that were never removed — a weekly automated diff between the running config and the last-approved baseline catches this early. SAQ A assessments typically rely on the service provider's controls, but SAQ D merchants must produce their own ruleset evidence and the quarterly review log.
exampleEvidence: Palo Alto NGFW running configuration export (XML) dated within 6 months, with a completed business justification form for every inbound rule permitting traffic to the CDE, signed by the network owner
Network access control matrix spreadsheet mapping each permitted traffic flow to source, destination, protocol, port, business justification, and quarterly review date
Quarterly firewall rule review record showing each rule examined, reviewer name, outcome (retain/modify/remove), and sign-off date
Automated configuration drift report from Tufin or AlgoSec comparing the current running config to the last approved baseline, showing zero unapproved changes
Deny-all default policy screenshot from the firewall management console showing the final rule in each CDE-bound policy is an explicit deny-all with logging enabled
Change-management closure records for the last quarter showing every firewall rule addition or removal was approved prior to implementation

title: Network access to cardholder data environment restricted
description: Network access to and from the cardholder data environment is restricted.
guidance: Place the CDE in an isolated network segment with only explicitly required inbound and outbound paths permitted.
implementationGuide: Produce a current-state network segmentation diagram that explicitly labels every path into the CDE and identifies the control enforcing that boundary — this is the artefact QSAs ask for first. Use Illumio Core or Guardicore (now Akamai Segmentation) to generate a workload-communication map and verify no undocumented east-west paths exist between the CDE and out-of-scope systems. Every permitted inbound path must be justified, time-limited where possible, and terminated at a DMZ — not directly into a CDE host. 'Done' means the segmentation diagram is current within 30 days, the automated workload map confirms no unlisted communications, and a penetration test from outside the CDE to a CDE host returns no direct paths. The most common QSA finding is a management VLAN or backup channel that bridges the CDE to a flat corporate network — enumerate every VLAN, not just production ones. ROC assessments require evidence from all system components; SAQ A merchants may rely on the service provider's segmentation evidence supplemented by a scope-reduction attestation.
exampleEvidence: Network segmentation diagram (approved within 30 days) showing CDE boundaries, all inbound/outbound permitted paths, DMZ placement, and the specific control device enforcing each boundary
Illumio Core or Guardicore workload-communication map export showing all active flows into and out of CDE workloads, with no undocumented east-west paths flagged
VLAN and subnet inventory listing every network segment, its classification (CDE/out-of-scope/connected), and the access control list restricting cross-segment traffic
Internal penetration test report section demonstrating that a tester starting outside the CDE could not traverse directly to a CDE host without passing through a documented control point
Firewall rule export showing DMZ-to-CDE rules are restricted to specific destination IPs and ports with no wildcard destinations
Semi-annual network segmentation review record signed by the network architect confirming all CDE boundaries remain correctly enforced

title: Network connections between trusted and untrusted networks controlled
description: Network connections between trusted and untrusted networks are controlled.
guidance: Deploy stateful inspection firewalls and IPS at all CDE boundaries and log all connection attempts.
implementationGuide: Deploy stateful inspection firewalls between every trusted and untrusted network boundary and verify they drop all inbound connections not initiated from the trusted side — run a packet-capture test to confirm, not just a policy review. Palo Alto NGFW with Zone Protection profiles or Check Point with IPS blades are appropriate platforms; configure anti-spoofing on every interface. Document each trusted/untrusted network pair in a boundary register and assign an owner responsible for reviewing the corresponding ruleset. 'Done' means a packet-capture from an untrusted zone shows only responses to outbound-initiated sessions crossing the boundary, and the boundary register has been reviewed in the last six months. QSAs frequently catch environments where an 'internal' firewall between two corporate segments is treated as fully trusted with no stateful inspection — every segment adjacent to the CDE is an untrusted network until proven otherwise. SAQ D and ROC assessments require per-boundary evidence; SAQ A relies on the service provider demonstrating this control.
exampleEvidence: Check Point or Palo Alto NGFW stateful inspection policy export showing anti-spoofing enabled on all CDE-adjacent interfaces and explicit rules blocking inbound unsolicited traffic
Packet-capture file (Wireshark .pcap) and analysis report demonstrating that an unsolicited inbound TCP SYN from an untrusted zone is dropped at the boundary firewall
Network boundary register listing every trusted/untrusted network pair, the device enforcing the boundary, the assigned owner, and the date of last rule review
IPS/IDS alert report from the boundary device for the past 30 days, showing the system is actively inspecting traffic and logging events
Anti-spoofing configuration screenshot from the firewall management console showing reverse-path forwarding or equivalent controls enabled on untrusted interfaces
Semi-annual boundary review sign-off document listing each boundary examined, reviewer, findings, and remediation actions taken

title: Processes for managing vendor defaults defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for applying secure configurations to all system components are defined and understood.
guidance: Adopt a hardening standard (e.g. CIS Benchmarks) for each system type and document exceptions through a risk acceptance process.
implementationGuide: Pull a full inventory of every vendor-supplied default account, password, and configuration parameter across all in-scope system components before any other hardening step — you cannot change what you haven't catalogued. Use CIS Benchmarks (CIS-CAT Pro) as the baseline for each operating system, database, and network device type, and map each benchmark recommendation to the corresponding vendor default you are overriding. Automate compliance checking with Chef InSpec or Puppet Bolt so that every new system provisioned is assessed against the hardening standard before it reaches production. 'Done' means CIS-CAT Pro scans show a compliance score of at least 80% against the relevant benchmark for every in-scope component, and no vendor default credentials remain on any device. The most common QSA catch is a network appliance or out-of-band management interface left with factory credentials because it wasn't in the original asset inventory — include every interface, not just primary management ports. SAQ A merchants should verify their hosted service provider applies this control; SAQ D and ROC environments must produce scan evidence for every component type.
exampleEvidence: CIS-CAT Pro scan report for each in-scope OS and database type, dated within 90 days, showing benchmark compliance score and a remediation plan for any failed checks
System hardening standard document referencing the applicable CIS Benchmark version, listing each vendor default changed and the replacement configuration applied
Asset inventory extract showing every in-scope system component, its OS version, and the hardening standard assigned to it, reviewed within the last 12 months
Privileged account audit report confirming no vendor default usernames (e.g., 'admin', 'sa', 'root' with default password) exist on any in-scope device
Change record from the system build process showing the hardening checklist was completed and signed off before each new system was admitted to the production CDE
Interview record with the system administrator documenting the process for reviewing vendor security advisories and updating the hardening standard when new defaults are discovered

title: System components configured and managed securely
description: System components are configured and managed securely.
guidance: Change all vendor-supplied default credentials before deploying any system component into the CDE.
implementationGuide: Run a CIS-CAT Pro or Qualys Policy Compliance scan against every in-scope system component and remediate every critical finding before the next assessment cycle — not after. For server builds, use a golden image pipeline (via HashiCorp Packer or AWS EC2 Image Builder) that bakes in the hardening standard so new instances inherit a compliant baseline automatically. Disable all unnecessary services, protocols, and daemons at the OS level and document each disabled item with its justification in the hardening record. 'Done' means the latest Policy Compliance scan shows no critical or high findings against the CIS Benchmark on any CDE host, and the build pipeline produces only golden-image-based instances. QSAs repeatedly find development or test services (e.g., Telnet, FTP, SNMP v1) still running on production CDE hosts because they were enabled during a troubleshooting session and never turned off — enforce an automated service inventory check on every weekly scan. ROC assessments require scan evidence for every unique system type; SAQ D requires at minimum a sample set covering each OS and application tier.
exampleEvidence: Qualys Policy Compliance or CIS-CAT Pro scan report for every unique CDE system type, dated within 90 days, with findings grouped by severity and a signed remediation plan for highs and criticals
Golden image build manifest from HashiCorp Packer or AWS EC2 Image Builder showing the hardening steps applied and the benchmark version used, with a dated approval signature
Running-services inventory for a representative CDE host (output of 'ss -tlnp' or equivalent) showing only documented, business-justified services are active
Hardening standard configuration document listing every disabled service, protocol, and daemon, with the business justification for each disablement
Automated compliance drift alert from Qualys or Chef InSpec showing a new finding was raised within 24 hours of an out-of-baseline service being enabled on a CDE host during testing
Change-management record showing the hardening standard was reviewed and updated within the last 12 months, with approval from the security team

title: Wireless environments configured and managed securely
description: Wireless environments are configured and managed securely.
guidance: Use WPA3 or WPA2-Enterprise with 802.1X for all wireless networks in or near the CDE; scan quarterly for rogue access points.
implementationGuide: Start by running a wireless scanner (using Ekahau or AirMagnet Enterprise) across all facilities that contain or are adjacent to the CDE to detect every access point — authorised and rogue — before documenting anything else. Segment all wireless networks from the CDE with a stateful firewall; no wireless SSID should have a direct routed path into the CDE without traversing an explicit control boundary. Change all wireless vendor defaults (SSID names, pre-shared keys, admin credentials, SNMP community strings) and replace WEP or WPA with WPA3-Enterprise using 802.1X with RADIUS authentication. 'Done' means the wireless scanner shows zero detected APs not in the authorised AP register, and a firewall policy export confirms no wireless subnet has a direct route to any CDE host. QSAs routinely find legacy WPA2-PSK SSIDs used for IoT or printer networks that sit on the same VLAN as CDE systems — include all wireless networks in scope regardless of stated purpose. SAQ A merchants with no wireless in their environment must document that fact; SAQ D and ROC assessments require a quarterly wireless scan.
exampleEvidence: Ekahau or AirMagnet Enterprise wireless site-survey report dated within 90 days, listing every detected access point with MAC address, SSID, encryption type, and authorisation status
Authorised wireless AP register showing each AP's MAC address, location, SSID, encryption standard (WPA3-Enterprise), and last configuration review date
RADIUS server configuration export confirming 802.1X authentication is required for all corporate wireless SSIDs adjacent to or connected to the CDE
Firewall policy export showing the wireless network subnet has no direct routing to the CDE — all traffic passes through an explicit stateful inspection rule
WPA3 or WPA2-Enterprise configuration screenshot from the wireless controller (e.g., Cisco WLC or Aruba Central) showing PSK-only SSIDs are not present in the CDE-adjacent environment
Quarterly wireless scan log showing the scan date, personnel conducting the scan, access points detected, and disposition of any newly found APs

title: Storage of account data minimised
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting stored account data are defined and understood.
guidance: Implement a data retention policy that deletes cardholder data when no longer needed for business or legal purposes.
implementationGuide: Conduct a data-discovery scan across all in-scope systems using Spirion (formerly Identity Finder) or Ground Labs Enterprise Recon to locate every stored PAN, SAD element, or sensitive authentication data before making any retention decisions. Map every data flow that results in card data being written to disk, including transaction logs, error logs, application caches, and backup files — developers consistently underestimate where card data lands. Apply a documented data retention policy that specifies the maximum retention period for each data category, backed by automated deletion or tokenisation for data beyond that period. 'Done' means the latest data-discovery scan returns zero unencrypted PAN hits outside of approved storage locations, and every identified storage location has a documented retention period and deletion schedule. QSAs commonly find PANs in application error logs or in database query result caches that nobody knew about — schedule monthly discovery scans, not annual ones. SAQ A merchants processing via fully hosted payment pages may still have PAN in server-side logs if misconfigured; SAQ D and ROC assessments require evidence of the full data-discovery programme.
exampleEvidence: Spirion or Ground Labs Enterprise Recon data-discovery scan report dated within 30 days, showing zero unencrypted PAN or SAD hits outside of approved storage locations, or a remediation log for any hits found
Data retention and disposal policy document specifying the maximum retention period for each card data category (PAN, SAD, CVV, track data), with approval signature and review date
Application data-flow diagram annotated to show every point where card data is written to disk, including logs, caches, and database tables, with the retention control applied at each point
Automated deletion or tokenisation job run log showing scheduled removal of card data beyond the retention period, with completion timestamps and record counts deleted
Database schema extract and DBA interview record confirming that no SAD elements (full track, CVV2, PIN) are stored in any table after authorisation completes
Evidence of a quarterly data-minimisation review: a signed checklist showing each data store was examined, the amount of card data found, and the disposal action taken

title: SAD not retained after authorisation
description: Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored after authorisation.
guidance: Verify through technical testing that PANs, CVVs, and track data are purged from all storage after transaction completion.
implementationGuide: Run a direct SQL query or data-discovery tool (Ground Labs Enterprise Recon or Spirion) against every database and data store that participates in the authorisation flow to confirm that no SAD — full track data, CVV2/CVC2, or PIN blocks — remains after authorisation completion. Work with application developers to add a post-authorisation assertion in the code that verifies SAD fields are null or empty before writing any transaction record to persistent storage. Review every third-party payment SDK and gateway integration to confirm the library does not buffer SAD in memory beyond the authorisation response. 'Done' means a data-discovery scan run 24 hours after a batch of test transactions finds zero SAD in any persistent store, and the code review confirms the post-authorisation assertion is present. QSAs find SAD retention most often in debug log files generated by payment application middleware — disable verbose logging in production or add a log-scrubbing filter. For SAQ A, the service provider must attest; SAQ D and ROC assessments require direct database evidence and a code or configuration review.
exampleEvidence: Ground Labs Enterprise Recon or Spirion scan report run after a test transaction batch, confirming zero track data, CVV2, or PIN block values found in any database or file system
Application source code review extract (or Checkmarx SAST report) showing the post-authorisation logic explicitly nullifies SAD fields before writing the transaction record
Database schema and sample row export from the transactions table, with SAD columns shown as null or absent after authorisation, reviewed by the DBA and signed off
Payment SDK/gateway vendor attestation or documentation confirming that SAD is not persisted beyond the authorisation response within their library
Application error log sample from production showing that verbose payment-field logging is disabled and no SAD appears in log entries
Code-change record showing the removal of any historical SAD storage logic, with the developer sign-off and QA test confirmation

title: SAD encrypted during authorisation
description: Sensitive authentication data is encrypted when stored during the authorisation process.
guidance: Use strong encryption (AES-256) for any temporary SAD storage during authorisation and ensure keys are protected in an HSM.
implementationGuide: Confirm with your payment application vendor or development team that SAD transmitted during the authorisation process is encrypted in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher, and that no SAD is written to any intermediate store — including message queues, application servers, or API gateways — in cleartext. Use Burp Suite Pro or OWASP ZAP to intercept and inspect all payment API calls during integration testing to verify no SAD appears in query strings, headers, or unencrypted request bodies. Apply field-level encryption at the point of entry (e.g., using Adyen's client-side encryption or Stripe.js) so SAD never reaches your server in cleartext. 'Done' means Burp Suite Pro shows every payment API call using TLS 1.2+ and no SAD visible in any intercepted HTTP payload, and the application's data-flow documentation confirms no SAD touches any intermediate persistent store. A common failure mode is a logging proxy or web application firewall that logs full request bodies — verify WAF log retention settings exclude payment fields. SAQ A relies on the hosted provider's controls; SAQ D requires application-level testing evidence.
exampleEvidence: Burp Suite Pro or OWASP ZAP proxy log from an integration test session confirming all payment API calls use TLS 1.2 or higher and no SAD fields appear in any intercepted plaintext payload
TLS configuration report (e.g., from SSL Labs or testssl.sh) for all payment endpoints showing TLS 1.2+ enforced, TLS 1.0/1.1 and SSLv3 disabled
Application data-flow diagram showing SAD entry point, encryption applied at source (e.g., Adyen client-side encryption library), and confirmation that no server-side component receives cleartext SAD
Message queue or API gateway configuration export confirming payload logging is disabled or filtered for fields containing payment card data
Vendor documentation or attestation from the payment SDK provider confirming SAD is encrypted before leaving the browser or terminal and is not accessible to the merchant's server
QA test report from an authorisation integration test, signed by the lead developer, confirming SAD fields are not present in any server-side log, queue message, or temporary file

title: PAN secured wherever stored
description: Access to displays of full PANs and ability to copy PANs are restricted.
guidance: Mask PANs in all displays and reports (show only first 6 and last 4 digits); restrict copy-paste of full PANs.
implementationGuide: Implement tokenisation via a validated tokenisation service (Stripe, Braintree, or a dedicated vault such as VGS — Very Good Security) or one-way hashing with a keyed HMAC so that PANs stored for recurring billing are never held in your own database in cleartext. For systems that must store PAN directly, deploy an HSM (nShield or Thales Luna) to perform all encryption and key operations — never store encryption keys on the same system as the encrypted PAN. Maintain a key register documenting each key's ID, algorithm, creation date, expiry, and custodian, reviewed quarterly. 'Done' means a database export of the cardholder data table shows only tokens or encrypted ciphertext in the PAN column, the HSM key usage log shows all encryption operations were performed by the HSM, and the key register is current. QSAs regularly find PAN stored in plaintext in a 'legacy' reporting database that was excluded from the tokenisation migration — include reporting and analytics databases in the discovery scan. ROC assessments require key management procedure evidence; SAQ A merchants offloading storage entirely to the provider must document that no PAN reaches their systems.
exampleEvidence: Database export (sanitised) of the cardholder data table showing only tokens (format-preserving or non-PAN tokens) or encrypted ciphertext in the PAN field, with no plaintext PAN present
HSM (nShield or Thales Luna) key usage log extract showing all PAN encryption and decryption operations were performed by the HSM, not by application-layer software
Encryption key register listing each active key's identifier, algorithm (AES-256), creation date, planned expiry, and named custodians, signed and reviewed within the last quarter
Tokenisation service integration documentation (e.g., VGS vault configuration or Stripe token API call log) confirming PAN is exchanged for a token before storage in the merchant's database
Ground Labs Enterprise Recon data-discovery scan report confirming zero plaintext PAN in any database, file system, or backup archive in scope
Key management procedure document describing key generation, distribution, storage, rotation, and destruction processes, with the last annual review sign-off

title: PAN secured with strong cryptography
description: Primary account number is secured with strong cryptography wherever it is stored.
guidance: Encrypt PANs at rest using AES-256; manage keys using a formal key management procedure with an HSM.
implementationGuide: Deploy AES-256 encryption for all stored PAN, implemented through an HSM (nShield or Thales Luna Network HSM) that never exposes key material outside the hardware boundary — application code should call the HSM API, not hold keys in memory. Define a key management lifecycle: generation inside the HSM, dual-custodian key ceremony for any export, maximum 12-month operational key validity, and a documented destruction procedure using NIST SP 800-88 guidance. Run a cryptographic configuration review using a tool such as SSL Labs or a custom script to verify no deprecated algorithms (3DES, RC4, DES) are used anywhere in the encryption stack. 'Done' means the HSM audit log shows all key operations, the key register shows no key past its expiry date, and a code scan (Semgrep or Checkmarx) shows no hardcoded encryption keys in application code. QSAs frequently find keys embedded in application configuration files or source code repositories — scan all git repos with a tool like TruffleHog or GitGuardian. SAQ D and ROC require key management procedure walkthroughs; SAQ A merchants with outsourced storage only need provider attestation.
exampleEvidence: HSM (nShield or Thales Luna) configuration and audit log extract confirming AES-256 is the algorithm used for PAN encryption and all key operations are performed within the hardware boundary
Key management procedure document covering the full key lifecycle: generation, distribution, storage, rotation (maximum 12-month period), and destruction per NIST SP 800-88
Key register showing each active encryption key's identifier, algorithm, creation date, expiry date, custodian names, and date of last review, with no expired keys present
Semgrep or Checkmarx SAST scan report confirming no hardcoded encryption keys or cryptographic secrets found in the application source code or configuration files
TruffleHog or GitGuardian scan result for all in-scope git repositories, dated within the last month, showing zero encryption key secrets detected
Dual-custodian key ceremony record (sign-in sheet or video evidence reference) for the most recent key generation event, listing the two custodians and the date

title: Processes for protecting account data in transit defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting cardholder data during transmission over open, public networks are defined and understood.
guidance: Document all pathways where cardholder data is transmitted and enforce TLS 1.2+ on each.
implementationGuide: Document every transmission path where PAN or SAD travels — including internal service-to-service calls, third-party API integrations, and batch file transfers — before configuring any encryption control. Use Qualys SSL Labs API or testssl.sh to scan all external-facing payment endpoints and confirm TLS 1.2 as the minimum, with TLS 1.3 preferred and all older protocol versions explicitly disabled. For internal service-to-service calls within a microservices architecture, enforce mutual TLS (mTLS) using a service mesh such as Istio or HashiCorp Consul. 'Done' means every transmission path in the data-flow document has a corresponding SSL Labs grade of A or A+ and an mTLS configuration record where applicable. A recurring QSA finding is a monitoring or batch-transfer channel (e.g., SFTP to a processor) that still accepts TLS 1.0 because a legacy system hasn't been upgraded — inventory every client certificate and TLS version negotiated. SAQ A merchants must confirm their payment service provider uses TLS 1.2+; SAQ D and ROC assessments require direct scanning evidence of all in-scope transmission paths.
exampleEvidence: SSL Labs or testssl.sh scan report for every external-facing payment endpoint, dated within 90 days, showing TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.0/1.1 and SSLv3 disabled, and a grade of A or A+
Data-flow diagram annotating every PAN/SAD transmission path (internal and external) with the protocol version, certificate details, and the system owner responsible for each endpoint
Istio or HashiCorp Consul mTLS policy export confirming mutual TLS is enforced on all internal service-to-service calls within the CDE microservices environment
SFTP or file-transfer gateway configuration export showing TLS 1.2+ is the only negotiated protocol for batch file transfers to the payment processor
Certificate inventory listing all TLS certificates on in-scope endpoints, including issuing CA, validity dates, and key length (minimum RSA-2048 or ECDSA-256)
Incident or change record showing TLS 1.0/1.1 was explicitly disabled on all in-scope endpoints, with a before/after configuration diff and approver sign-off

title: PAN protected during transit
description: PAN is protected with strong cryptography during transmission over open, public networks.
guidance: Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1 on all endpoints; enforce TLS 1.2+ and strong cipher suites; use certificate pinning where possible.
implementationGuide: Map every point-to-point PAN transmission — card-present terminal to acquirer, e-commerce checkout to payment gateway, and internal processor-to-processor calls — and confirm each uses TLS 1.2 or higher with a valid certificate from a trusted CA. For point-of-interaction (POI) devices, verify the encryption is point-to-point (P2PE) using a PCI-validated P2PE solution (e.g., Ingenico's P2PE or Shift4 Payments) so that PAN is encrypted at the swipe/dip/tap and never reaches the merchant's application in cleartext. Use Wireshark or Burp Suite Pro during integration testing to verify no PAN appears in plaintext on the network at any hop. 'Done' means a Wireshark capture of a live test transaction shows only TLS-encrypted ciphertext at every hop and the P2PE solution listing on the PCI SSC website confirms the solution is currently validated. QSAs find PANs in cleartext most often in internal network captures between an application server and a backend database — test internally, not just at the perimeter. SAQ A-EP merchants must cover their own payment page submission; ROC assessments require evidence for every transmission path.
exampleEvidence: Wireshark packet capture from an internal network segment during a test transaction, with analysis confirming no PAN appears in plaintext at any hop between the POI device and the acquirer
PCI SSC P2PE solution listing printout or URL reference confirming the P2PE solution in use is currently validated, with the solution name, version, and listing date
TLS certificate details for all payment gateway endpoints, including issuing CA, key algorithm, validity period, and a confirmation that the certificate is not self-signed
Burp Suite Pro proxy log from an e-commerce checkout test, showing the card data submission is encrypted in transit and no PAN is visible in any HTTP request or response
Network access control configuration showing the segment carrying payment traffic between the application server and database is isolated and monitored for cleartext card data
Third-party payment gateway integration agreement or attestation confirming the gateway uses TLS 1.2+ for all cardholder data transmissions on behalf of the merchant

title: Processes to protect all systems against malware defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting all systems against malware are defined and understood.
guidance: Develop and publish an anti-malware policy covering all in-scope system types, including exception handling.
implementationGuide: Define a formal anti-malware programme policy that specifies which system types require an anti-malware agent, the required scan frequency, the update cadence for signatures, and the response procedure when a threat is detected — publish this before deploying any tooling. Deploy CrowdStrike Falcon or SentinelOne on every Windows, Linux, and macOS system in the CDE; for systems where agents are not supported (some legacy POS), document the compensating controls and the risk acceptance. Configure the anti-malware platform to generate centralised alerts to your SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) and set alerting thresholds that trigger an incident ticket within 15 minutes of detection. 'Done' means the CrowdStrike or SentinelOne management console shows 100% agent deployment across all in-scope hosts, zero hosts with outdated definitions (older than 24 hours), and a SIEM alert test confirms the 15-minute SLA. QSAs frequently catch systems added to the CDE mid-year that never had an agent deployed — reconcile the CMDB against the anti-malware console weekly. SAQ A merchants depend on the provider's controls; SAQ D and ROC require console evidence showing coverage and update status.
exampleEvidence: CrowdStrike Falcon or SentinelOne management console export showing 100% agent deployment across all in-scope CDE hosts, with agent version and last definition update timestamp for each host
Anti-malware programme policy document specifying which system types require an agent, scan frequency, update cadence, and incident-response steps, with the last annual review sign-off
SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) alert rule configuration showing anti-malware events are ingested and an automated ticket is created within 15 minutes of a threat detection
Weekly CMDB-to-anti-malware console reconciliation report showing zero in-scope hosts without an agent, dated within the last 7 days
Exception register listing any systems where an anti-malware agent cannot be installed, the compensating controls in place, and the risk acceptance sign-off by the CISO
Sample incident ticket generated by the SIEM following a test anti-malware detection event, showing detection time, alert time, and ticket creation time to confirm the 15-minute SLA

title: Malware threats continually addressed
description: Malware threats are continually addressed.
guidance: Deploy EDR/AV on all CDE systems with real-time protection; update signatures and engines at least daily.
implementationGuide: Configure your anti-malware platform (CrowdStrike Falcon or Carbon Black) to run in prevention mode — not just detection mode — on all CDE hosts, and validate that prevention is active by reviewing the policy applied to each host group in the management console. Establish a vulnerability management integration so that newly published malware indicators of compromise (IoCs) from threat-intelligence feeds (e.g., MISP or Recorded Future) are ingested and acted on within 24 hours. Schedule full-disk scans at least weekly on every CDE host and retain scan results for 12 months as audit evidence. 'Done' means the anti-malware console shows prevention mode active on all in-scope hosts, the threat-intelligence feed is confirmed live, and the scan history shows no week in the past 12 months with a missed scan. QSAs find detection-only deployments frequently — a policy set to 'alert' rather than 'block' on a critical server is a finding. ROC assessments require evidence of the threat-intelligence integration; SAQ D requires at minimum weekly scan logs.
exampleEvidence: CrowdStrike Falcon or Carbon Black policy export showing prevention mode is active (not detection-only) on the host group containing all CDE systems
Weekly full-disk scan log for all in-scope CDE hosts covering the past 12 months, showing scan completion status, number of threats detected, and remediation actions taken

Threat-intelligence feed integration configuration (e.g., MISP or Recorded Future connector) showing IoCs are ingested and applied to the anti-malware platform, with the last successful sync timestamp
Anti-malware management console screenshot showing zero CDE hosts with definition signatures older than 24 hours, captured at the time of assessment
Incident record for the most recent detected threat on a CDE host, showing detection time, automated prevention action, analyst review, and closure
Anti-malware policy review record signed by the security team lead confirming the prevention policy was reviewed and confirmed appropriate within the last 12 months

title: Anti-malware mechanisms and processes active
description: Anti-malware mechanisms and processes are active, maintained, and monitored.
guidance: Configure anti-malware to log all detections centrally and alert the SOC on any malware detection in the CDE.
implementationGuide: Verify in the anti-malware management console (CrowdStrike Falcon or Symantec Endpoint Protection) that every in-scope host has the agent running, definitions updated within 24 hours, and real-time protection enabled — pull this as a scheduled report, not a one-time screenshot. Implement tamper protection so that an end user or malware cannot disable the agent; in CrowdStrike this is the 'Sensor Tamper Protection' policy, and it must be assigned to all CDE host groups. Forward all anti-malware events to the SIEM (Splunk PCI DSS app or QRadar) and create correlation rules that alert when an agent goes offline or tamper protection is disabled. 'Done' means the scheduled anti-malware health report shows 100% coverage, tamper protection enabled, and definitions current, with SIEM correlation rules verified by a test event in the last 30 days. QSAs catch environments where a host was excluded from tamper-protection policies 'temporarily' for a maintenance window and the exclusion was never reversed. SAQ D and ROC require the tamper-protection policy assignment as evidence.
exampleEvidence: CrowdStrike Falcon or Symantec Endpoint Protection scheduled health report showing 100% of in-scope hosts with agent running, real-time protection enabled, and definitions updated within 24 hours
Tamper-protection policy assignment screenshot from the anti-malware console showing all CDE host groups have tamper protection enabled with no exclusions
Splunk PCI DSS app or QRadar dashboard screenshot showing anti-malware events are being ingested in real time with an alert rule active for agent-offline and tamper-disabled events
Test alert record confirming the SIEM raised an incident ticket within 15 minutes of a simulated tamper-protection disable event on a test CDE host
Anti-malware exclusion register listing any file-path or process exclusions applied on CDE hosts, with business justification and security team sign-off for each
Monthly anti-malware compliance report from the management console covering the last 3 months, showing no week where coverage dropped below 100% on in-scope hosts

title: Anti-phishing mechanisms in place
description: Anti-phishing mechanisms protect users against phishing attacks.
guidance: Deploy email filtering with anti-phishing and sandboxing capabilities; run quarterly phishing simulations.
implementationGuide: Deploy an email security gateway with anti-phishing capabilities — Microsoft Defender for Office 365 Plan 2 or Proofpoint Targeted Attack Protection — and configure DMARC, DKIM, and SPF records for all domains that send or receive email on behalf of the organisation. Enable anti-impersonation and URL-rewriting features so that phishing links are detonated in a sandbox before delivery; confirm the sandbox verdict is enforced, not just advisory. Train all personnel who handle cardholder data with phishing simulation campaigns (KnowBe4 or Cofense) quarterly, targeting CDE-adjacent staff specifically. 'Done' means DMARC is set to 'p=reject', the email gateway blocks or quarantines 100% of emails failing DMARC, and the last quarterly phishing simulation shows a click-through rate under 5% for CDE staff. A common QSA catch is DMARC set to 'p=none' — monitoring-only does not satisfy the control. SAQ A merchants with managed email should verify provider DMARC posture; SAQ D and ROC require simulation reports and gateway configuration evidence.
exampleEvidence: DMARC DNS record and DMARC reporting aggregate (RUA) report showing the policy is set to 'p=reject' and the reporting is active, with the report dated within 30 days

Microsoft Defender for Office 365 or Proofpoint TAP configuration export showing anti-impersonation, URL-rewriting, and sandbox detonation policies are active and enforced (not advisory) for all in-scope email domains
KnowBe4 or Cofense phishing simulation campaign report for the most recent quarter, showing click-through rate, reporting rate, and repeat offenders for CDE-adjacent staff
SPF and DKIM DNS record exports for all organisational domains, with a validation test result (e.g., MXToolbox output) confirming correct configuration
Email gateway quarantine report for the last 30 days showing the volume of phishing emails blocked, the detection method used, and zero phishing emails delivered to CDE staff
Security awareness training completion records showing all personnel with CDE access completed the phishing module within the last 12 months

title: Processes to protect against vulnerabilities defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting all systems and networks against vulnerabilities are defined and understood.
guidance: Establish a vulnerability management programme with defined SLAs for patching by severity.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal vulnerability management policy and assign a named owner for the programme before running any scans — the policy must specify discovery frequency, severity SLAs (e.g., critical within 15 days, high within 30 days), and the escalation path for missed SLAs. Use Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io as the scanning platform and configure authenticated scans on all in-scope CDE hosts to get accurate CVE coverage. Integrate vulnerability findings with your ticketing system (Jira or ServiceNow) so every finding creates a remediation ticket automatically and SLA compliance is tracked. 'Done' means Qualys VMDR shows zero open critical CVEs older than 15 days on any CDE host, and the Jira SLA dashboard shows less than 5% of vulnerability tickets missed their target closure date in the last quarter. QSAs consistently find authenticated scanning is not configured on some hosts — unauthenticated scans miss a large proportion of findings and will not satisfy a QSA. ROC assessments require authenticated scan reports; SAQ D requires quarterly scan results with remediation tracking.
exampleEvidence: Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io authenticated vulnerability scan report for all in-scope CDE hosts, dated within 90 days, showing zero open critical CVEs beyond the 15-day SLA
Vulnerability management policy document specifying scan frequency, severity-based remediation SLAs, escalation procedures, and named programme owner, reviewed within the last 12 months
Jira or ServiceNow vulnerability remediation ticket dashboard export showing open ticket age by severity, with SLA compliance metrics for the last quarter
Scan configuration extract from Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io showing authenticated scan credentials are configured for all in-scope host types
Risk acceptance record for any vulnerability not remediated within SLA, with the business justification, compensating controls, and CISO sign-off
Quarterly vulnerability management review meeting minutes, listing the top 10 open vulnerabilities, owners, remediation status, and agreed closure dates

title: Bespoke and custom software protected
description: Bespoke and custom software are developed securely.
guidance: Integrate SAST, SCA, and DAST into the CI/CD pipeline; train developers on OWASP Secure Coding Practices.
implementationGuide: Integrate SAST into the CI/CD pipeline using Semgrep or Checkmarx and configure it to fail the build on any critical or high security finding before code reaches a staging environment — do not allow bypassing the gate without an explicit security team waiver. Mandate a threat model for every new payment-related feature using STRIDE or OWASP Threat Dragon; the threat model output must be reviewed and signed by the security architect before the feature is scheduled for development. Apply secure coding training (SANS Secure Coding or OWASP AppSec courses) to all developers with access to payment code, and track completion in your LMS. 'Done' means the CI/CD pipeline shows zero deployments to the CDE in the last quarter that bypassed the SAST gate, every new payment feature has a signed threat model, and all developers show LMS completion within the last 12 months. QSAs find CI/CD bypass mechanisms ('emergency deploy' pipelines without the SAST gate) used routinely — every deployment path must pass through the same security gate. ROC and SAQ D assessments require pipeline configuration evidence and the threat-model artefacts.
exampleEvidence: CI/CD pipeline configuration (e.g., Jenkinsfile or GitHub Actions workflow YAML) showing the Semgrep or Checkmarx SAST step is a mandatory gate that fails the build on critical/high findings, with no bypass mechanism for CDE-bound deployments
Semgrep or Checkmarx scan report for the most recent release to the CDE, showing all critical and high findings were resolved before deployment, with the developer and security reviewer sign-offs
Threat model document (STRIDE or OWASP Threat Dragon output) for the most recent payment feature, showing identified threats, mitigations applied, residual risk, and security architect approval
LMS completion report showing 100% of developers with CDE code access completed an approved secure coding course within the last 12 months
Security team waiver log showing any SAST-gate bypasses in the last quarter, with justification, compensating controls, and CISO approval — ideally showing zero waivers
Code review checklist records from the last 5 CDE deployments, showing each submission was reviewed by a second developer and a security team member before merge

title: Security vulnerabilities identified and addressed
description: Security vulnerabilities are identified and addressed.
guidance: Subscribe to PCI SSC and vendor advisories; patch Critical CVEs within 30 days and High within 60 days.
implementationGuide: Subscribe to CVE feeds (NVD, vendor advisories, and CISA KEV) and triage new vulnerabilities against your asset inventory within 24 hours of publication — assign a named owner to this triage process. Run Qualys VMDR or Rapid7 InsightVM authenticated scans quarterly at minimum, with monthly scans on internet-facing CDE components, and track every finding in a remediation register. Apply patches to critical CVEs (CVSS 9.0+) within 15 days and high CVEs (CVSS 7.0–8.9) within 30 days, with documented SLA exceptions requiring CISO sign-off. 'Done' means the remediation register shows zero critical CVEs open beyond 15 days, the scanning platform shows 100% of in-scope hosts have been scanned within the required window, and patch deployment is confirmed by a rescan. QSAs find patch SLAs treated as targets rather than deadlines — build escalation procedures for missed deadlines into the vulnerability management policy. ROC assessments require patch deployment evidence (change records) in addition to scan reports; SAQ D requires quarterly scan results.
exampleEvidence: Qualys VMDR or Rapid7 InsightVM authenticated scan report for all CDE hosts, dated within 30 days for internet-facing components and 90 days for internal, showing all critical and high CVEs with remediation status
Vulnerability remediation register showing each open finding's CVE ID, CVSS score, affected host, assigned owner, due date, and current status, with zero criticals open beyond 15 days
CISA KEV subscription confirmation and a triage log showing each new KEV entry was reviewed against the asset inventory within 24 hours, with the disposition documented
Change-management records for the last quarter showing patch deployments to CDE hosts with deployment date, patch version, and a rescan confirmation showing the CVE is closed
Risk acceptance records for any vulnerability not remediated within SLA, with compensating controls, business justification, and CISO sign-off
Patch management dashboard export from Qualys VMDR showing mean time to remediate (MTTR) by severity for the past 12 months

title: Public-facing web applications protected
description: Public-facing web applications are protected against attacks.
guidance: Deploy a WAF in front of all public-facing CDE web applications; review and tune rules quarterly.
implementationGuide: Deploy a web application firewall (WAF) — AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF, or Imperva WAP — in blocking mode in front of every public-facing payment application and verify it is actively blocking OWASP Top 10 attack patterns, not just logging them. Run an application-layer vulnerability scan using Burp Suite Pro or Qualys WAS against all public-facing payment pages quarterly, and a full penetration test at least annually. For e-commerce environments, implement a client-side Content Security Policy (CSP) and Subresource Integrity (SRI) to prevent skimming-style script injections. 'Done' means the WAF is in blocking mode, the last Burp Suite Pro scan shows zero critical or high findings unresolved, and the CSP header is present on all payment pages with a 'strict-dynamic' policy. QSAs find WAFs in 'detection only' mode routinely — changing the mode to blocking requires careful rule tuning but is mandatory. ROC assessments require WAF configuration evidence and the full pen test report; SAQ A-EP requires WAF evidence for the payment page host.
exampleEvidence: AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF, or Imperva WAP configuration export showing the ruleset is in blocking mode and OWASP Core Rule Set (CRS) is applied to all payment application endpoints
Burp Suite Pro or Qualys WAS application vulnerability scan report for all public-facing payment pages, dated within 90 days, showing zero unresolved critical or high findings
Annual penetration test report (application layer) for the public-facing payment application, showing the testing scope, methodology, all findings, and remediation evidence
HTTP response headers capture from all payment pages confirming a Content-Security-Policy header with 'strict-dynamic' or equivalent and Subresource Integrity attributes on all third-party scripts
WAF alert log for the last 30 days showing blocked attack attempts, with a confirmation that no rule exceptions have been added for payment page URLs without security team approval
WAF rule change log showing every modification to the payment application ruleset in the last 12 months, with the change approver and a post-change test confirmation

domain: Req 7 — Restrict Access by Business Need
title: Processes for restricting access defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for restricting access to system components and cardholder data by business need to know are defined and understood.
guidance: Document an access control policy enforcing need-to-know and least privilege for all CDE access.
implementationGuide: Define a formal access control policy that specifies the principle of least privilege for every role accessing CDE systems — every permission must map to a documented job function, not to an individual. Use a role-based access control (RBAC) model implemented via CyberArk PAM or BeyondTrust Privileged Remote Access to manage and record all privileged access to CDE systems, with session recording enabled. Conduct a quarterly access review using an IGA tool (SailPoint IdentityNow or Saviynt) to certify that every account's permissions remain appropriate. 'Done' means the IGA tool shows 100% of CDE access certifications completed in the last quarter, no account has permissions beyond its defined role, and CyberArk or BeyondTrust shows every privileged session was recorded. QSAs consistently find privileged accounts shared between multiple administrators — every administrator must have a unique named account. ROC assessments require the full access review cycle; SAQ D requires quarterly certification records.
exampleEvidence: SailPoint IdentityNow or Saviynt quarterly access certification report showing 100% of CDE accounts were reviewed, certified or revoked, with reviewer names and completion dates
CyberArk PAM or BeyondTrust Privileged Remote Access session recording log for the last quarter, showing all privileged access to CDE systems was recorded and the recordings are retained
Access control policy document defining the RBAC model, the principle of least privilege, and the quarterly review requirement, with the last annual review sign-off
Role definition matrix mapping each CDE system role to its specific permissions and the job functions authorised to hold that role, reviewed by the CISO
User account audit report for all CDE systems showing no shared or generic privileged accounts and no accounts with permissions beyond their defined role
Evidence of the most recent access revocation — an IGA ticket showing a departed employee's CDE access was removed within 24 hours of their exit, with the manager's request and IT confirmation

domain: Req 7 — Restrict Access by Business Need
title: Access to system components and data appropriately defined
description: Access to system components and data is appropriately defined and assigned.
guidance: Implement RBAC for all CDE systems and review roles and permissions at least every six months.
implementationGuide: Build an access matrix for every CDE system that maps each user role to the specific data objects and functions it can access — this is the artefact a QSA will request first during an access-control review. Implement the matrix using directory-based RBAC (Active Directory security groups or Okta groups) and assign permissions at the group level, never at the individual user level. Run SailPoint IdentityNow or One Identity Manager to detect and report on access that falls outside the matrix definitions (segregation-of-duties conflicts and excessive privilege). 'Done' means every CDE account can be traced back to an entry in the access matrix, SailPoint shows zero SoD violations active on CDE systems, and the matrix was reviewed and approved in the last 12 months. QSAs find 'power users' or local administrator accounts that were granted broad access for a project and never scoped down — the access matrix review process must include date-of-last-use analysis. ROC assessments require the full matrix and IGA evidence; SAQ D requires the matrix and quarterly review records.
exampleEvidence: CDE access matrix document listing every role, the specific systems and data objects each role can access, and the job functions authorised to hold each role, approved within the last 12 months
Active Directory or Okta group membership export showing all CDE-system permissions are assigned via named security groups, with no direct user-level permission assignments
SailPoint IdentityNow or One Identity Manager SoD conflict report showing zero active segregation-of-duties violations on CDE systems
Quarterly access review record showing each CDE account was reviewed against the access matrix, with reviewer names, dates, and outcomes (retain/modify/revoke)
Last-logon date report for all CDE accounts showing no dormant accounts (no login in 90+ days) that retain active access permissions
Privilege escalation request and approval log for the last quarter showing any temporary elevated access was granted with a defined expiry date and was revoked on schedule

domain: Req 7 — Restrict Access by Business Need
title: Access to system components and data managed via access control systems
description: Access to system components and data is managed via an access control system(s).
guidance: Centralise access management through a PAM or IAM system with automated provisioning tied to HR data.
implementationGuide: Configure your IAM platform (Okta, Azure AD, or CyberArk PAM) to enforce access controls programmatically — access must be denied by default and granted only through an approved provisioning workflow, never by direct system configuration changes. Implement just-in-time (JIT) privileged access using CyberArk PAM or BeyondTrust so that elevated permissions are granted for a defined time window and automatically revoked. Run automated access recertification campaigns quarterly so that managers are prompted to confirm or revoke their team members' access to CDE systems. 'Done' means the IAM platform shows access is provisioned only through approved workflow tickets, CyberArk JIT shows all privileged sessions had a defined expiry enforced, and the access recertification completion rate is 100% for CDE-system owners. QSAs find IT staff who retain local admin accounts on CDE servers 'just in case' — these bypass JIT controls and must be removed. ROC assessments require evidence of the provisioning workflow and the JIT enforcement for every privileged access type.
exampleEvidence: CyberArk PAM or BeyondTrust JIT access log showing privileged sessions on CDE systems were granted for defined time windows and automatically revoked at expiry, with no open-ended elevated sessions
Okta or Azure AD provisioning workflow configuration showing new CDE access requests require manager approval and security team review before the account is activated
Quarterly access recertification campaign completion report from SailPoint or Okta showing 100% of CDE system owners certified their team members' access
IAM audit log extract for the last quarter showing zero directly-modified user permissions on CDE systems outside of the approved provisioning workflow
JIT access request and approval log for the last quarter showing each privileged session request, the approver, the time window granted, and the automatic revocation confirmation
Dormant account report showing any CDE account with no activity in 90 days was automatically disabled, with the disable action logged in the IAM platform

domain: Req 8 — Identify Users and Authenticate Access
title: Processes for identifying users defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for identifying and authenticating access to system components are defined and understood.
guidance: Define an identity and authentication policy aligned to PCI-DSS requirements for all CDE users.
implementationGuide: Create a user lifecycle management procedure that covers the full cycle: account provisioning, modification, suspension, and deletion — and assign ownership of this procedure to a named HR/IT joint team. Implement automated account lifecycle hooks between your HR system (Workday or SAP SuccessFactors) and your IAM platform (Okta or Azure AD) so that a joiner or leaver event in the HR system triggers an IAM provisioning or de-provisioning workflow within 24 hours. Log every account creation and deletion with the approver's name and the business justification in a centralised identity audit log. 'Done' means the joiner-leaver-mover integration is live, the IAM audit log shows zero CDE accounts that were active more than 24 hours after an exit date in the HR system, and the lifecycle procedure has been reviewed in the last 12 months. QSAs routinely find accounts of contractors or service accounts that remain active months after the engagement ended — include non-employee accounts in the same lifecycle process. ROC assessments require the lifecycle procedure, integration configuration, and the IAM audit log.
exampleEvidence: Workday or SAP SuccessFactors HR system leaver-trigger configuration and corresponding Okta or Azure AD de-provisioning workflow, showing the integration is active and the maximum time between HR exit date and account disable is 24 hours
IAM audit log extract for the last quarter showing all CDE account provisioning and de-provisioning events with approver name, date, and business justification
User lifecycle management procedure document covering joiner, mover, and leaver processes for employees and contractors, with named owners and the last annual review sign-off
Evidence of a recent leaver case: HR exit date, the IAM de-provisioning ticket, and the CDE account disable confirmation, all timestamped within 24 hours
Contractor and service-account register showing all non-employee CDE accounts, their associated contract end dates, and last review date, with zero accounts active beyond their end date
Orphaned account scan report from SailPoint or a custom AD query, dated within 30 days, showing zero CDE accounts with no associated active identity record in the HR system

domain: Req 8 — Identify Users and Authenticate Access
title: User identification and authentication managed
description: User identification and related accounts for users and administrators are strictly and thoroughly managed throughout an account's lifecycle.
guidance: Assign unique IDs to all CDE users; disable accounts within 24 hours of departure; never share credentials.
implementationGuide: Enforce unique user IDs for every individual accessing any CDE system component — no shared accounts, no group logins — and configure this as a technical control in Active Directory or Okta, not just as a policy statement. Deploy a password management standard requiring a minimum of 12 characters, complexity, no reuse of the last 13 passwords, and a maximum 90-day expiry for non-MFA accounts; use CyberArk PAM for all privileged account credential management. Audit all authentication configurations quarterly using an identity governance tool (SailPoint IdentityNow) to detect shared accounts, accounts with non-expiring passwords, or disabled complexity requirements. 'Done' means the Active Directory audit report shows 100% of CDE accounts meet the password policy, zero shared accounts exist, and CyberArk manages every privileged credential. QSAs frequently find service accounts with non-expiring passwords and broad permissions — every service account must have a documented owner and a rotation schedule. SAQ D and ROC require authentication policy configuration evidence for every system component type.
exampleEvidence: Active Directory or Okta password policy export showing 12-character minimum, complexity enabled, 13-password history enforced, and 90-day maximum age for all CDE user accounts

CyberArk PAM privileged account report showing every privileged CDE account credential is managed, vaulted, and rotated according to the defined schedule, with no credentials managed outside the vault
SailPoint IdentityNow identity audit report showing zero shared accounts, zero accounts with non-expiring passwords, and zero accounts with disabled complexity requirements on CDE systems
Service account register listing every service account on CDE systems, its purpose, owner, password rotation schedule, and last rotation date, reviewed quarterly

Sample account provisioning record showing a new CDE user account was created with a unique username, the password policy enforced at creation, and the user forced to set a new password on first login
Failed-login attempt report from the SIEM for CDE systems over the last 30 days, showing account lockout policies are triggering correctly after the defined number of failed attempts

domain: Req 8 — Identify Users and Authenticate Access
title: User authentication for CDE systems enforced
description: User authentication for users and administrators is established and managed via an authentication policy and procedures.
guidance: Enforce MFA for all non-console administrative access and all remote access to the CDE.
implementationGuide: Configure authentication controls on every CDE system so that accounts lock after no more than 10 consecutive failed login attempts, session timeouts trigger after no more than 15 minutes of inactivity, and all authentication events are logged. Use CyberArk PAM for all privileged account authentication — no direct OS-level login with privileged credentials should be possible outside of the PAM workflow. Verify these controls are implemented at the system level (not just the application level) by reviewing OS and application authentication settings separately. 'Done' means a lockout test on a CDE system confirms the account locks after 10 failed attempts, a session idle test confirms timeout at 15 minutes, and the SIEM shows all authentication events are being ingested. QSAs find lockout policies configured on the application but not on the underlying OS, allowing brute-force at the OS level — test both layers. SAQ D and ROC require per-component authentication configuration evidence; SAQ B-IP requires authentication controls on POI devices.
exampleEvidence: Active Directory or local OS security policy export for CDE systems showing account lockout threshold set to 10 or fewer attempts, lockout duration of at least 30 minutes, and session idle timeout of 15 minutes or less
CyberArk PAM configuration showing all privileged account authentication to CDE systems is routed through the PAM platform and direct OS-level login with privileged credentials is blocked
Authentication lockout test record showing a test account on a CDE system was locked after 10 failed login attempts, with the test date, tester, and system tested documented
Session idle timeout test record showing a CDE system session was terminated after 15 minutes of inactivity, with the test date, tester, and system tested documented
SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) authentication event ingestion confirmation showing all CDE system login events — successful and failed — are being collected and retained
Application-level authentication configuration screenshot from the payment application admin console showing lockout and timeout policies matching the OS-level configuration

domain: Req 8 — Identify Users and Authenticate Access
title: MFA implemented for access into CDE
description: Multi-factor authentication is implemented to secure access into the CDE.
guidance: Require MFA at the network and application layer for all users accessing the CDE; do not allow single-factor bypass.
implementationGuide: Deploy MFA for all non-console access into the CDE using an approved MFA solution — Duo Security, Okta Verify, or Microsoft Authenticator with Conditional Access — and verify MFA is enforced at the network access layer (VPN or ZTNA), not just at the application level. Configure Conditional Access policies in Azure AD or Okta to require MFA for all authentication attempts targeting CDE systems, with no IP-based exclusions for internal networks. Test MFA enforcement by attempting CDE access from a known internal IP without completing MFA — access should be denied. 'Done' means the Okta or Azure AD Conditional Access policy shows MFA is required for all CDE access with no exceptions, and an access attempt without MFA from any source returns an authentication failure. QSAs find 'trusted network' exceptions that allow bypassing MFA from the corporate LAN — these exceptions are not permitted under PCI DSS v4.0. ROC and SAQ D assessments require policy configuration evidence and an MFA enforcement test.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Azure AD Conditional Access policy export showing MFA is required for all authentication to CDE systems with no IP-based or network-based exclusions
Duo Security or Microsoft Authenticator enrollment report showing 100% of users with CDE access have an MFA method enrolled and active
MFA enforcement test record showing an attempted login to a CDE system from an internal corporate IP without completing MFA was denied, with the test date, tester, and system documented
VPN or ZTNA gateway configuration showing MFA is enforced at the network access layer before a session to the CDE is established
MFA bypass exception register showing zero active exceptions for CDE system access, or any exceptions with documented business justification, compensating controls, and CISO sign-off
Authentication event log from Okta or Azure AD for the last 30 days showing all successful CDE authentications included an MFA factor, with no MFA-skipped sessions

domain: Req 8 — Identify Users and Authenticate Access
title: Shared and generic accounts managed
description: Use of application and system accounts and associated authentication factors is strictly managed.
guidance: Eliminate shared accounts in the CDE; where service accounts are required, manage credentials in a PAM vault with automated rotation.
implementationGuide: Identify every shared or generic account on all CDE systems (service accounts, application accounts, vendor accounts, emergency break-glass accounts) and document each one in an account register with a named owner, the business justification, and the access control preventing its use for interactive sessions. Manage all shared account credentials in CyberArk PAM or HashiCorp Vault, configured to rotate passwords after every use for service accounts and after every break-glass activation. Disable or remove any shared account that cannot be justified — do not leave accounts 'just in case'. 'Done' means the account register is complete, every account in the register has a named owner, CyberArk manages all credentials, and an IAM audit confirms no interactive sessions have been established using service accounts in the last 90 days. QSAs routinely find vendor accounts left enabled with the vendor's default credentials after an implementation project — audit vendor accounts quarterly. ROC assessments require session recording evidence for break-glass account use.
exampleEvidence: Shared and generic account register for all CDE systems listing account name, system, business justification, named owner, access restriction method, and last review date

CyberArk PAM or HashiCorp Vault configuration showing all shared account credentials are vaulted, with automatic rotation configured after each use for service accounts and after each break-glass activation
IAM audit log confirming no interactive (human) sessions were established using service accounts on CDE systems in the last 90 days

Break-glass account activation log for the last 12 months showing each activation, the authorising manager, the purpose, the session recording reference, and the post-use password rotation confirmation
Vendor account audit report showing all vendor accounts on CDE systems, their current status (active/disabled), and a confirmation that any account not needed for active work is disabled
Quarterly shared-account review sign-off document showing each shared account was reviewed, the justification confirmed, and any accounts without justification were disabled

title: Processes for restricting physical access defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for restricting physical access to cardholder data are defined and understood.
guidance: Document physical access controls for all CDE locations and review the policy annually.
implementationGuide: Write a physical access control policy that defines who is authorised to access each physical location containing CDE systems or media, the mechanism controlling access (card reader, PIN pad, biometric), and the process for granting, reviewing, and revoking access. Conduct a physical access control audit using your physical security system (Lenel S2, Genetec, or HID Global) quarterly — pull an access rights report and certify every entry against the list of authorised personnel. Review visitor access logs weekly and confirm that every visitor entry has a corresponding authorisation record. 'Done' means the physical access system audit shows every badge holder with CDE facility access is on the approved list, no former employees retain access, and visitor logs have no unaccounted entries. QSAs commonly find that badge access is never revoked for employees who changed roles — link badge access to HR role changes. ROC assessments require physical access audit evidence for every CDE facility; SAQ B and SAQ C require evidence for the card-acceptance area.
exampleEvidence: Genetec or Lenel S2 physical access rights report for the CDE facility, dated within 90 days, showing every active badge holder with access, their role, and the date access was last reviewed
Physical access control policy document defining authorised access roles for each CDE location, access mechanisms, and the review and revocation process, with the last annual review sign-off
Quarterly physical access certification record showing each badge holder's access to the CDE facility was reviewed and confirmed or revoked, with reviewer signatures
Visitor access log for the last 30 days showing every visitor entry, the host employee, the purpose, entry and exit times, and a confirmation that every entry has a corresponding authorisation
Evidence of a recent access revocation for a departing employee: the HR exit date, the badge deactivation record, and the time between the two, confirming revocation within 24 hours
Physical access control system configuration screenshot showing the CDE area requires two-factor physical access (e.g., badge plus PIN) and all access events are logged

title: Physical access controls manage entry into facilities and systems
description: Physical access controls manage entry into facilities and systems containing cardholder data.
guidance: Implement badge access to all CDE areas; maintain access logs and review them monthly.
implementationGuide: Install electronic access control readers (HID Global card readers or Suprema biometric units) on all entry points to areas containing CDE systems or media and configure them to log every access event with a timestamp and the badge/biometric ID. Verify the access control system enforces a least-privilege model — only job-function-justified personnel have access to each zone, not all employees. Test each physical access control mechanism quarterly: confirm the reader rejects an invalid badge, a revoked badge, and an after-hours access attempt by an authorised user outside their permitted schedule. 'Done' means every CDE entry point has an electronic reader, the quarterly test results pass all scenarios, and the access log shows no after-hours anomalies without an authorisation record. QSAs catch propped-open doors and tailgating in facilities with high foot traffic — couple electronic access control with a door-open alarm and review door-held-open events weekly. SAQ B and ROC assessments require physical inspection evidence; document all testing.
exampleEvidence: Physical access control system map showing the location of every reader on CDE area entry points, with the reader type, zone designation, and access group assigned
Quarterly physical access control test records showing results of invalid badge, revoked badge, and after-hours access tests for each CDE entry point, signed by the physical security manager
Door-open and door-held-open alarm log for the last 30 days showing each event was investigated and resolved within the defined SLA
Access control badge issuance log for the last quarter showing every new badge issued for CDE areas, the authorising manager, job function justification, and activation date
Physical access event log for the CDE area for the last 7 days showing all entry events with timestamp and badge ID, with a weekly review sign-off from the physical security team
CCTV/video surveillance coverage map showing all CDE entry points are within camera view, with a retention configuration screenshot confirming recordings are kept for at least 90 days

title: Physical access for personnel and visitors authorised and managed
description: Physical access for personnel and visitors is authorised and managed.
guidance: Require all visitors to the CDE to sign in, be escorted, and wear a visible time-limited badge.
implementationGuide: Implement a visitor management system (Envoy or Proxyclick) to log every visitor entering a CDE area: capture name, organisation, host, purpose, date/time in, date/time out, and a badge number. Require all visitors to be escorted at all times within the CDE area and brief the escorting employee on their responsibility to prevent the visitor from accessing systems or media. For personnel, run a quarterly reconciliation of badge access rights against the current authorised-access list, removing any access for personnel who changed roles or left. 'Done' means the visitor management system shows 100% of visitor entries have complete records, no visitor was unescorted in the CDE area based on badge event sequence analysis, and the quarterly personnel access reconciliation is signed off. QSAs find visitor logs with missing exit times or unverified identities — require government-issued ID verification at the reception point. ROC assessments require full visitor log evidence and an escort policy walkthrough.
exampleEvidence: Envoy or Proxyclick visitor management system export for the last 30 days showing all CDE visitor entries with name, organisation, host, purpose, badge number, entry time, and exit time — no incomplete records
Escort policy document defining the requirement for all visitors to be escorted within the CDE, the escort's responsibilities, and the process for managing pre-authorised vendor access
Personnel CDE access reconciliation record for the last quarter showing all badge holders were compared against the HR authorised-access list and any discrepancies were resolved
Visitor badge design and issuance record confirming visitor badges are visually distinct from employee badges and are collected at the exit point
