Government ID verification log or visitor sign-in sheet showing visitors provided photo ID before being admitted to the CDE area
Physical security incident log entry for any case where an unescorted visitor or access anomaly was detected, showing the detection method, response, and corrective action

title: Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed
description: Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.
guidance: Log all media movements, store media in locked containers, and use certified destruction services with certificates.
implementationGuide: Classify all media containing cardholder data (hard drives, USB drives, backup tapes, paper printouts) and store it in a physically secured, access-controlled location — a locked cabinet with a key register or an electronic access-controlled cage. Use Iron Mountain or a similar certified media destruction service to dispose of physical media, and require a certificate of destruction for every disposal event. Maintain a media inventory log that tracks every piece of media from creation through storage to destruction, updated within 24 hours of any media movement. 'Done' means the media inventory log accounts for every piece of CDE media with no unresolved gaps, every destruction event has a vendor certificate, and the storage location access log shows no unauthorised entries in the last quarter. QSAs consistently find that decommissioned hard drives were disposed of through general IT recycling without a destruction certificate — the destruction process must cover all media types, including SSDs. ROC assessments require the full media inventory and destruction certificates; SAQ D requires a media handling procedure.
exampleEvidence: Media inventory log listing every piece of media containing cardholder data (type, identifier, location, status, custodian), updated within 24 hours of any media movement, reviewed quarterly
Iron Mountain or equivalent certified media destruction service certificates for all CDE media disposed of in the last 12 months, showing the media identifier, destruction method, and date
Locked storage area access log for the last 30 days showing all access to the CDE media storage location with timestamp and badge ID, and no unauthorised entries
Media handling and destruction policy document defining classification, storage, transportation, and destruction requirements for all media types, with the last annual review sign-off
Transportation authorisation record for any CDE media moved outside the secure storage area, showing the authorising manager, destination, purpose, and return confirmation
Quarterly media inventory audit record showing each item in the inventory was physically verified, discrepancies resolved, and the audit was signed off by the media custodian

title: POI devices are protected from tampering and substitution
description: Point-of-interaction (POI) devices are protected from tampering and unauthorised substitution.
guidance: Maintain a POI device inventory and inspect devices for tampering at least annually or after suspicious events.
implementationGuide: Create a POI device inventory register listing every card-acceptance terminal — serial number, model, location, assigned employee, and date of last physical inspection — and inspect each device at every shift change or at minimum weekly to check for skimmers, unauthorised attachments, or device substitution. Train all customer-facing staff who handle POI devices on how to identify a skimming device, the reporting procedure, and what to do if a device is suspected to have been tampered with. Use a tamper-evident seal or security label on each POI device and photograph the seal state at each inspection. 'Done' means the POI device inventory is current, every device has an inspection record within the last 7 days, and staff training records show 100% completion for all card-acceptance personnel. QSAs find POI inventories that haven't been updated when devices were replaced or moved — treat a missing serial number as an incident. SAQ B and SAQ C assessments require full POI inventory and inspection evidence.
exampleEvidence: POI device inventory register listing every terminal's serial number, model, physical location, assigned employee, last inspection date, and seal/label status, reviewed monthly
Shift-change or weekly POI device inspection log for the last 30 days showing each device, the inspector's name, the date and time, the seal integrity result, and any anomalies noted
Tamper-evident seal inspection photographs for a representative sample of POI devices, dated within the last 7 days, showing the seal state and device serial number
Staff training completion records showing all personnel who handle POI devices completed the anti-skimming and device-inspection training module within the last 12 months
POI device incident report for any suspected tampering event in the last 12 months, showing detection method, response actions, device quarantine, and outcome
POI device manufacturer security documentation or PCI PTS listing confirming the terminal model in use is a currently approved PCI PTS device

title: Processes for logging and monitoring defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring all access to system components and cardholder data are defined and understood.
guidance: Define a logging policy specifying what events must be captured in the CDE and the required retention period.
implementationGuide: Define a logging and monitoring policy specifying which events must be logged on every CDE system, the required retention periods, the SIEM platform to be used, and the review frequency — this policy is the foundation before any technical implementation. Deploy the Splunk PCI DSS app or IBM QRadar and onboard all in-scope CDE sources (firewalls, servers, databases, applications) before the next assessment cycle. Configure at minimum the following event types: all authentication (success and failure), privilege escalation, system and application configuration changes, access to cardholder data, and security event alerts. 'Done' means the Splunk or QRadar log source inventory shows 100% of in-scope CDE components are onboarded, and the logging policy's required event types are confirmed in the ingested event stream. QSAs find databases not sending logs to the SIEM because no one prioritised the integration — database audit logging (Oracle Audit Vault or Microsoft SQL Server Audit) is a separate configuration that must be explicitly enabled. ROC assessments require log source coverage evidence; SAQ D requires SIEM evidence.
exampleEvidence: Splunk PCI DSS app or QRadar log source inventory showing 100% of in-scope CDE components are onboarded and sending logs, with the last successful event timestamp for each source
Logging and monitoring policy document specifying required event types, retention periods, SIEM platform, and review frequency, with the last annual review sign-off
Sample log entries from a CDE system showing authentication, privilege escalation, configuration change, and cardholder data access events are being captured with correct timestamps
Database audit configuration report (Oracle Audit Vault or SQL Server Audit) confirming database-level authentication and data-access events are being logged and forwarded to the SIEM
Log source gap analysis report showing any in-scope system not yet onboarded to the SIEM, with a remediation plan and target onboarding date
SIEM alert rule inventory showing active rules for all required PCI DSS event categories, with the last test date and result for each rule

title: Audit logs capture all individual user access
description: Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.
guidance: Enable logging of all access, authentication events, and privileged actions on all CDE systems; centralise in a SIEM.
implementationGuide: Enable audit logging on every CDE system component at the OS, application, and database level to capture all individual user access — including read access to cardholder data tables. Use Oracle Audit Vault or Microsoft SQL Server Audit for database-level capture, and configure OS-level logging via Windows Event Forwarding or Linux auditd, then forward all events to Splunk or QRadar. Verify that logs capture at minimum: user ID, event type, date/time, success/failure indicator, and origination point for every access event. 'Done' means a Splunk search for any cardholder data table access in the last 24 hours returns events with all required fields populated, and a test access to the CHD table by a test user is visible in the SIEM within 5 minutes. A common QSA finding is read access to the cardholder data store not being captured — SELECT queries on CHD tables are frequently not logged by default. ROC assessments require demonstration of log capture for all required event types; SAQ D requires database audit evidence.
exampleEvidence: Oracle Audit Vault or SQL Server Audit configuration report showing audit policies are enabled for all SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on cardholder data tables
Splunk or QRadar search result showing all cardholder data table access events for the last 24 hours, with user ID, event type, timestamp, and source IP fields populated
Linux auditd or Windows Event Forwarding configuration export showing OS-level authentication and privileged-command events are being captured and forwarded to the SIEM
Test access log: a documented test where a named test account accessed a CHD table and the corresponding event appeared in Splunk/QRadar within 5 minutes, with the test date and result
SIEM log source health report showing all CDE systems are sending events within the expected frequency threshold, with no gaps longer than 15 minutes
Application audit log configuration screenshot showing the payment application logs all user access to payment records, including read-only access, with the required fields

title: Audit logs protected from destruction and unauthorised modifications
description: Audit logs are protected from destruction and unauthorised modifications.
guidance: Ship logs to an immutable store separate from production systems; prevent log deletion by application accounts.
implementationGuide: Configure your SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) and log management platform to store logs in a write-once or WORM-protected repository that prevents modification or deletion by any account, including administrators. Implement log integrity controls using cryptographic hashing (e.g., Splunk's index integrity feature or a separate SIEM module) so that any tampering with stored log files can be detected. Restrict access to the logging infrastructure: only designated SIEM administrators should have write access, and all access to the SIEM admin console should itself be logged and reviewed. 'Done' means the SIEM stores logs in a WORM-protected store, an integrity check on a sample of archived logs passes, and the SIEM admin access log shows only authorised personnel accessed the platform in the last quarter. QSAs find that log retention policies are defined but the technical control enforcing WORM protection was never actually enabled — verify the storage configuration, not just the policy. ROC assessments require SIEM storage configuration evidence and an integrity check demonstration.
exampleEvidence: Splunk index configuration or QRadar log source storage configuration showing WORM or write-once protection is enabled on the log archive, with the storage system type and retention period
Log integrity verification test result showing a hash check on a sample of archived CDE logs passed, confirming no tampering occurred, with the test date and method documented
SIEM administrator access control configuration showing only designated personnel have write access to the logging infrastructure, with MFA required for admin console login
SIEM admin console access log for the last quarter showing all logins with username, timestamp, action taken, and confirmation that all access was by authorised personnel
Log retention configuration screenshot showing the SIEM retains at least 12 months of logs, with at least 3 months immediately available for query and 9 months in archival storage
Offsite or cloud log replication configuration showing CDE logs are replicated to a geographically separate storage location to prevent single-point-of-failure loss

title: Audit logs reviewed
description: Audit logs are reviewed to identify anomalies or suspicious activity.
guidance: Review CDE logs daily using automated correlation rules; escalate anomalies to the SOC within defined SLAs.
implementationGuide: Define and document log review procedures specifying which event types are reviewed daily, by whom, and what constitutes an alert requiring escalation. Use the Splunk PCI DSS app or QRadar UBA (User Behavior Analytics) to automate detection of anomalous access patterns and surface them as high-priority alerts; do not rely on manual log review for high-volume event streams. Assign daily log review responsibilities to a named analyst and require a signed daily review record. 'Done' means a daily review record exists for every day in the last 30 days, the Splunk/QRadar alert queue shows all critical alerts were acknowledged within the defined SLA, and no alert sat unreviewed for more than 24 hours. QSAs find that log review records are created but the reviewer wasn't actually looking at the logs — test by injecting a known anomalous event and checking whether the reviewer's record references it. ROC assessments require 12 months of review records; SAQ D requires at minimum a daily review process for critical event types.
exampleEvidence: Daily log review records for the last 30 days, each signed by the reviewing analyst and documenting the event categories reviewed, anomalies found, and actions taken
Splunk PCI DSS app or QRadar UBA alert report for the last 30 days showing all high-priority alerts, the time between alert generation and analyst acknowledgement, and the resolution

Log review procedure document specifying which event types are reviewed daily, the review cadence for other event categories, the escalation path, and the analyst assigned, with the last review sign-off
Alert SLA compliance report showing the percentage of critical alerts acknowledged within the defined SLA (e.g., 4 hours) over the last quarter
Injected test anomaly record: evidence that a synthetic anomalous event was deliberately generated on a CDE system, and the reviewer's daily review record referenced the detection of that event
Quarterly log review quality assessment showing a sample of daily review records was tested for completeness and the analysts were able to identify injected anomalies

title: Audit log history retained and available for analysis
description: Retain audit log history for at least 12 months, with at least the most recent three months available for immediate analysis.
guidance: Configure SIEM retention for 12 months minimum with 3 months online/hot storage for immediate querying.
implementationGuide: Configure log retention in your SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) to retain a minimum of 12 months of CDE logs, with the most recent 3 months immediately queryable online and the remaining 9 months in warm/cold archival storage accessible within 24 hours. Use Amazon S3 with Object Lock (WORM mode) or Azure Immutable Blob Storage for archival to prevent deletion. Document the retention configuration and verify it is enforced technically — policy statements alone do not satisfy this control. 'Done' means a test query for a log event from 11 months ago returns a result within 24 hours, the S3 Object Lock configuration shows a minimum 12-month retention lock, and the SIEM shows no log gaps over the retention period. QSAs find retention policies that specify 12 months but the storage capacity was never sized to hold that volume — calculate daily log ingest volume and provision storage accordingly. ROC assessments require a retention configuration proof; SAQ D requires evidence that archived logs are recoverable.
exampleEvidence: Splunk index retention policy configuration or QRadar log source archive settings showing 12-month total retention with 3 months online and 9 months in archival storage
Amazon S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage configuration showing the WORM retention lock is set to a minimum of 12 months on the CDE log archive bucket
Log archive retrieval test record showing a log event from 10–11 months ago was successfully retrieved from archival storage within 24 hours, with the test date and the event details
SIEM storage capacity report showing current log ingest volume, projected growth, and available storage confirming the 12-month retention period can be maintained without capacity issues
Log gap analysis report from Splunk or QRadar for the last 12 months, showing no gaps in log ingestion from any in-scope CDE system longer than 15 minutes
Archival storage access log showing only SIEM administrators accessed the log archive, with no deletion events in the last 12 months

title: Failures of critical security controls detected, reported, and responded to promptly
description: Failures of critical security controls are detected, alerted, and addressed promptly.
guidance: Monitor health of critical security tools (AV, firewall, SIEM) and alert on any failures within 15 minutes.
implementationGuide: Define critical security controls whose failure must be detected and responded to — at minimum: anti-malware failure, IDS/IPS failure, FIM failure, audit logging failure, and physical access control failure — and build automated monitoring alerts in Splunk or QRadar for each. Configure health-check agents on each critical security tool so that a failure to report within a defined window generates an alert in the SIEM and a page to the on-call analyst. Test each alert by simulating a failure of each critical control quarterly and documenting the detection time. 'Done' means each defined critical control has a corresponding SIEM alert rule, every quarterly failure test was detected within the defined SLA, and no critical control failure went undetected for more than 15 minutes in the test. QSAs find alerting configured for external threats but not for the failure of internal security tools — the absence of a heartbeat is as important as a positive alert. ROC assessments require the alert rule inventory and the quarterly test records; SAQ D requires the monitoring configuration evidence.
exampleEvidence: Splunk or QRadar alert rule inventory listing each critical security control (anti-malware, IDS/IPS, FIM, audit logging, physical access) with the corresponding alert rule name, trigger condition, and notification target
Quarterly critical control failure simulation test records for the last 12 months, showing each control tested, the simulated failure method, the detection time, and the analyst response time
Health-check agent configuration for CrowdStrike Falcon or Tripwire Enterprise showing heartbeat intervals and the SIEM alert trigger condition for a missed heartbeat
SIEM alert queue extract showing a recent critical control failure alert was acknowledged by the on-call analyst within 15 minutes, with the alert timestamp and acknowledgement timestamp
Critical security control failure response procedure document defining the escalation path, containment steps, and recovery procedure for each monitored control type
Post-incident review record for any actual critical control failure in the last 12 months, showing detection time, response time, root cause, and corrective action

title: Processes to test security of systems and networks defined
description: Processes and mechanisms for regularly testing security of systems and networks are defined and understood.
guidance: Define a security testing programme covering vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, and ASV scans.
implementationGuide: Write a formal security testing policy that defines the scope, methodology, frequency, and responsible parties for every test type — ASV scans, internal vulnerability scans, penetration tests, and wireless scans — and get it approved before scheduling any tests. Assign a named owner for each test type and build the testing calendar into the compliance programme so tests are not skipped when operationally inconvenient. Maintain a testing results register that captures the date, scope, findings, remediation status, and retest results for every assessment. 'Done' means the testing policy exists, the calendar shows tests were executed on schedule for the last 12 months, and the results register has no open critical findings older than the defined remediation SLA. QSAs find test programmes that exist on paper but were skipped for several quarters due to resource constraints — automate scheduling and escalate missed tests as compliance incidents. ROC assessments require the full testing programme documentation; SAQ D requires quarterly vulnerability scan results and an annual penetration test.
exampleEvidence: Security testing policy document defining scope, methodology, frequency, and responsible parties for ASV scans, internal scans, penetration tests, and wireless scans, with the last annual review sign-off
Testing calendar for the last 12 months showing all scheduled tests with actual execution dates, confirming no tests were skipped, and a compliance manager sign-off
Testing results register for the last 12 months listing each test type, date, scope, findings count by severity, remediation status, and retest confirmation
Penetration testing firm engagement letter or SOW confirming the scope, methodology (e.g., PTES or OWASP), and tester qualification for the most recent annual pen test
ASV scan vendor contract or service agreement confirming the ASV is on the PCI SSC approved ASV list, with the most recent scan completion certificate
Escalation record showing a missed test was treated as a compliance incident, with root cause, corrective action, and rescheduled test date

title: Wireless access points managed
description: Wireless access points are managed and identified.
guidance: Scan for and detect rogue wireless access points at least quarterly and investigate any unauthorised devices immediately.
implementationGuide: Run a wireless access point scan quarterly across all facilities containing CDE systems using Ekahau Site Survey or AirMagnet Enterprise, and compare the results against the authorised AP register — any AP not in the register is a rogue and must be investigated immediately. Configure your network access control system (Cisco ISE or Aruba ClearPass) to detect and quarantine unauthorised wireless devices automatically. Maintain an authorised AP register with each AP's MAC address, SSID, location, and the employee responsible for it, reviewed quarterly. 'Done' means the quarterly scan shows zero detected APs not in the authorised register, Cisco ISE is configured to quarantine unauthorised wireless devices, and the AP register is current. QSAs find environments where the IT team believes they have no wireless but the scan detects employee hotspots or IoT device APs — every wireless signal is in scope. SAQ D and ROC assessments require quarterly scan results; SAQ A environments with no wireless should document that finding from the scan.
exampleEvidence: Ekahau or AirMagnet Enterprise quarterly wireless scan report for all CDE-containing facilities, dated within 90 days, showing all detected APs with MAC address, SSID, channel, and authorisation status
Authorised AP register listing every permitted access point with MAC address, SSID, physical location, responsible owner, and last quarterly review date
Cisco ISE or Aruba ClearPass network access control policy configuration showing unauthorised wireless devices are quarantined automatically upon detection
Rogue AP investigation record for any unauthorised AP detected in the last 12 months, showing detection date, investigation findings, remediation action, and confirmation the AP was removed
Wireless scan comparison report showing this quarter's scan results were compared to the authorised AP register with zero unmatched devices, signed by the network security manager
Facility floor plan annotated with the authorised AP locations, confirming the scan was conducted in all areas of the facility including break rooms, server rooms, and loading docks

title: External and internal vulnerabilities are regularly identified, prioritised, and addressed
description: External and internal vulnerabilities are regularly identified, prioritised, and addressed.
guidance: Conduct quarterly authenticated vulnerability scans; remediate Critical findings within 30 days.
implementationGuide: Run authenticated internal vulnerability scans quarterly on all CDE hosts using Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io, and run external scans monthly on all internet-facing CDE components; configure the scan credentials to enable full OS and application-layer discovery. Engage an ASV (Trustwave or Coalfire) for external quarterly scans and confirm each scan achieves a passing status before the attestation period closes — a failing scan means no attestation. Remediate all critical and high findings from each scan within the SLA defined in your vulnerability management policy, with a documented rescan confirming closure. 'Done' means the last four quarters of ASV scan reports all show a passing status, internal scan results show no open critical CVEs beyond the SLA, and every finding has a remediation ticket in Jira. QSAs find merchants who ran the ASV scan once to get a 'pass' but never rescanned after changes to the external attack surface — rescanning is required after any significant change. ROC assessments require all four quarterly ASV scan reports; SAQ D requires quarterly internal and external scan evidence.
exampleEvidence: ASV (Trustwave or Coalfire) external quarterly scan reports for the last 4 quarters, each with a 'passing' status certificate signed by the ASV, covering all internet-facing CDE IP addresses
Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io authenticated internal scan reports for the last 4 quarters, showing all in-scope CDE hosts scanned with authenticated credentials and findings by severity
Vulnerability remediation log for the most recent scan cycle showing each critical and high finding, the remediation action taken, the closure date, and the rescan confirmation
ASV scan scope confirmation document listing all external IP addresses and domains in scope, signed by the merchant's security manager and submitted to the ASV before each scan
Change-triggered rescan record showing an external ASV scan was re-run after a significant change to the internet-facing environment (e.g., new IP address added, WAF rules changed)
Internal scan credential configuration export from Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io confirming authenticated scan accounts are configured for each in-scope OS and database type

title: External and internal penetration testing regularly performed
description: External and internal penetration testing is regularly performed, and exploitable vulnerabilities and security weaknesses are corrected.
guidance: Conduct a full penetration test at least annually and after any significant infrastructure or application change.
implementationGuide: Engage a qualified penetration testing firm (CREST-certified or PCI SSC QSA-company affiliated) to conduct an annual penetration test covering all CDE system components from both external and internal perspectives, using a threat-intelligence-informed methodology. Scope the test to include all network-layer and application-layer attack vectors, segmentation testing to validate CDE isolation, and social engineering if the organisation processes cards in person. Remediate all critical and high findings from the penetration test within 90 days of the report date and obtain a retest to confirm remediation. 'Done' means the annual pen test report is from a qualified tester, covers all required scope including segmentation testing, and all critical and high findings have a closed retest report. QSAs frequently find penetration tests scoped to the external perimeter only, omitting internal segmentation tests — the internal perspective is mandatory under PCI DSS v4.0. ROC assessments require the full report, tester qualifications, and retest evidence.
exampleEvidence: Annual penetration test report from a CREST-certified or PCI SSC-qualified firm, showing tester qualifications, methodology, scope (external and internal), all findings with severity ratings, and a remediation summary
Segmentation test section of the penetration test report demonstrating that the tester attempted to traverse from out-of-scope systems to the CDE and was blocked at all tested paths
Penetration test scope agreement signed by the merchant and the testing firm, listing all in-scope IP addresses, systems, and application URLs, including internal CDE components
Critical and high finding remediation records from the most recent pen test, showing each finding, the remediation action taken, and the retest confirmation from the testing firm
Penetration testing firm's CREST certification or QSA company listing printout confirming the firm was qualified at the time of testing
Penetration test retest report from the same firm confirming all critical and high findings from the original test were retested and confirmed as remediated

title: Network intrusions and unexpected file changes detected and responded to
description: Network intrusions and unexpected file and system changes are detected and responded to.
guidance: Deploy IDS/IPS and file integrity monitoring (FIM) on all CDE systems; review alerts daily.
implementationGuide: Deploy a network intrusion detection system (Cisco Secure IDS or Snort/Suricata) on all CDE network segments and a file integrity monitoring (FIM) solution — Tripwire Enterprise or OSSEC — on all CDE hosts, configured to alert on unauthorised changes to critical system files and configurations. Configure IDS signatures to detect payment card data exfiltration patterns (e.g., PAN-format regex in outbound traffic) and tune the ruleset to minimise false positives while maintaining a high true-positive rate. Integrate all IDS and FIM alerts into the SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) with automated ticket creation. 'Done' means the SIEM shows IDS and FIM alert sources for all in-scope segments and hosts, a FIM integrity check test on a CDE host triggers an alert within 5 minutes, and the IDS ruleset was reviewed within the last 12 months. QSAs find FIM deployed but monitoring only a handful of directories — the monitored file list must cover all critical OS, application, and configuration files. ROC assessments require IDS coverage for every CDE network segment and FIM coverage for every CDE host.
exampleEvidence: Tripwire Enterprise or OSSEC FIM policy configuration showing the list of monitored directories and files on CDE hosts, confirming all critical OS, application, and configuration paths are included

FIM alert test record showing a deliberate modification to a monitored file on a CDE host triggered a SIEM alert within 5 minutes, with the test date, file modified, alert time, and analyst acknowledgement
Cisco Secure IDS or Suricata IDS ruleset export showing PAN-format detection rules are active for outbound traffic on all CDE network segments
Splunk or QRadar dashboard screenshot showing IDS and FIM alert sources for all in-scope CDE network segments and hosts are active and generating events
IDS ruleset review record from the last 12 months showing the ruleset was updated to include the latest threat intelligence signatures, signed by the security operations manager
IDS false-positive rate report for the last quarter showing the tuned ruleset produces a false-positive rate below the defined threshold (e.g., less than 10% of IDS alerts)

title: Unauthorised changes on payment pages are detected and responded to
description: Unauthorised changes on payment pages are detected and responded to.
guidance: Implement change detection (e.g. sub-resource integrity, CSP) on all payment pages and alert on any unauthorised modification.
implementationGuide: Deploy a payment page change-detection mechanism — either a purpose-built solution like Jscrambler, Reflectiz, or PerimeterX (HUMAN Security) — or a custom CSP report-only monitoring setup with real-time alerting to detect unauthorised script injection or modification on all payment pages. Configure the solution to monitor every third-party script loaded on the payment page and alert immediately if a new script is added, an existing script hash changes, or a script contacts an unauthorised external domain. Review the full list of authorised third-party scripts quarterly and remove any that are no longer required. 'Done' means the monitoring solution is active on all payment page URLs, an alert is generated within 5 minutes of any unauthorised script change, and the quarterly script inventory review is signed off. QSAs introduced this control specifically because of Magecart-style attacks — a payment page with a third-party tag manager loading multiple unmonitored scripts is the classic finding. SAQ A-EP and ROC assessments require this control on all merchant-hosted payment pages.
exampleEvidence: Jscrambler, Reflectiz, or HUMAN Security monitoring dashboard configuration showing all payment page URLs are monitored with real-time alerting enabled for new or modified scripts
Alert test record showing a deliberate script modification on a test payment page triggered an alert within 5 minutes, with the alert timestamp, modified script URL, and analyst notification record
Quarterly authorised third-party script inventory for all payment pages, listing each script's URL, hash, purpose, and vendor, reviewed and signed off by the security team
Content Security Policy header for all payment pages, showing only explicitly authorised script sources are permitted and a CSP report-uri is configured to collect violations
Payment page change-detection alert log for the last 30 days showing all script change events detected, their classification (authorised/unauthorised), and the response taken
Subresource Integrity (SRI) implementation evidence showing all third-party scripts on the payment page have integrity attributes, confirmed by a page source inspection report

title: Comprehensive information security policy established, published, maintained, and disseminated
description: A comprehensive information security policy that governs and provides direction for protection of the entity's information assets is known and current.
guidance: Publish an overarching information security policy reviewed annually and signed by senior management.
implementationGuide: Draft and publish a comprehensive information security policy that addresses all 12 PCI DSS requirement areas, assigns accountability to named roles, and is approved by the board or executive sponsor — this is a living document that must be reviewed annually, not a one-time compliance artefact. Use a GRC platform (Vanta, Drata, or Tugboat Logic) to manage policy version control, track review dates, and automate staff acknowledgement workflows. Ensure the policy explicitly addresses cardholder data handling, acceptable use, incident response, and third-party management. 'Done' means the GRC platform shows the policy was approved by the board within the last 12 months, 100% of in-scope staff have acknowledged it, and it references all 12 PCI DSS requirement areas. QSAs frequently find policies that haven't been updated since the original QSA engagement and no longer reflect the actual technology environment — the annual review must result in a substantive update, not just a re-signature. ROC assessments require the full policy set; SAQ A requires a subset focused on the outsourced elements.
exampleEvidence: Information security policy document covering all 12 PCI DSS requirement areas, with version control showing the most recent annual review date and the board or executive approval signature
Vanta, Drata, or Tugboat Logic GRC platform policy acknowledgement report showing 100% of in-scope staff acknowledged the current policy version within the last 12 months
Board or executive committee meeting minutes approving the current version of the information security policy, dated within the last 12 months
Policy version history log showing previous versions, review dates, and a summary of substantive changes made at each review cycle
Policy exception register listing any approved deviations from the security policy, with the exception reason, compensating controls, risk acceptance, and expiry date
Staff acknowledgement tracking report showing completion percentages by department, with follow-up records for any staff who had not acknowledged within the required timeframe

title: Acceptable use policies defined and implemented
description: Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined and implemented.
guidance: Publish an acceptable use policy covering all user-facing technology; require annual staff sign-off.
implementationGuide: Publish an acceptable use policy (AUP) that explicitly covers the use of CDE systems, cardholder data, internet and email access, removable media, and personal devices — and require every employee and contractor with CDE access to sign it before access is granted. Integrate the AUP acknowledgement into the onboarding workflow (via Workday or your HR system) so that no new employee completes access provisioning without first signing the AUP. Review and update the AUP annually, and re-obtain signatures from all current staff when significant updates are made. 'Done' means the HR/GRC system shows 100% of CDE staff have a current signed AUP on file, and new joiners cannot complete access provisioning without an AUP signature. QSAs find AUPs that are acknowledged at onboarding but never re-signed when the policy is updated — require annual re-acknowledgement, not just onboarding sign-off. ROC assessments require signature records for all personnel; SAQ D requires the AUP document and acknowledgement log.
exampleEvidence: Acceptable use policy document covering CDE system use, cardholder data handling, internet and email use, removable media, and personal device restrictions, with the last annual review sign-off
HR or GRC system AUP acknowledgement report showing 100% of current employees and contractors with CDE access have a signed AUP on file dated within the last 12 months
Onboarding workflow configuration (Workday or equivalent) showing AUP acknowledgement is a mandatory step before CDE access is provisioned to a new employee
Re-acknowledgement campaign record showing that when the AUP was last updated, all existing CDE staff were required to re-sign and completion was tracked to 100%
Contractor AUP acknowledgement log showing all third-party personnel with CDE access have a signed AUP, with the signature date and contract reference
AUP version history showing previous versions, review dates, and the material changes made at each annual review cycle

title: Risks identified, evaluated, and managed
description: Risks to the cardholder data environment are formally identified, evaluated, and managed.
guidance: Conduct a formal CDE risk assessment at least annually and document the results in the risk register.
implementationGuide: Conduct a formal risk assessment annually (or after any significant change) using a structured methodology — ISO 27005, NIST SP 800-30, or FAIR — and produce a risk register that documents every identified risk to cardholder data, its likelihood, impact, current controls, and residual risk. Use a GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC, or Drata) to maintain the risk register and track risk treatment actions to closure. Present risk register updates to the executive sponsor or risk committee quarterly. 'Done' means the risk register is current (updated within 12 months or after the last significant change), every high-residual-risk item has a documented treatment plan with a named owner and due date, and the quarterly review is minuted. QSAs find risk assessments that are conducted as a one-time exercise and never updated when the environment changes — a new payment channel or cloud migration must trigger a risk assessment update. ROC assessments require the risk assessment methodology documentation and the full risk register; SAQ D requires an annual risk assessment.
exampleEvidence: Annual risk assessment report using ISO 27005, NIST SP 800-30, or FAIR methodology, covering all in-scope CDE assets and processes, with identified threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, impact, and residual risk ratings
Risk register export from Archer, ServiceNow GRC, or Drata showing all identified risks to cardholder data, current controls, residual risk level, treatment plan, owner, and target closure date
Risk committee or executive sponsor quarterly meeting minutes showing risk register updates were presented, discussed, and accepted, with attendee list and date
Risk assessment trigger log showing a new risk assessment or update was initiated when a significant change occurred (e.g., new payment channel deployed, cloud migration completed)
Risk treatment plan for the top three residual risks, showing the chosen treatment (accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid), the controls applied, the owner, and the progress status
Risk assessment methodology document defining the scoring criteria, risk rating thresholds, treatment requirements by rating, and review cadence, approved by the CISO

title: PCI DSS compliance managed
description: PCI DSS compliance is managed.
guidance: Assign a PCI DSS programme owner, maintain a compliance calendar, and produce an annual self-assessment questionnaire or ROC.
implementationGuide: Assign a named executive (CISO or equivalent) as the accountable owner for PCI DSS compliance and establish a formal compliance programme with a steering committee that meets quarterly. Use a GRC platform (Vanta, Drata, or Tugboat Logic) to automate continuous control monitoring, track evidence collection deadlines, and produce a real-time compliance dashboard. Define a compliance calendar with all required activities (quarterly scans, annual pen tests, semi-annual reviews) and assign owners with escalation paths for missed deadlines. 'Done' means the steering committee has quarterly meeting records for the last 12 months, the GRC platform shows a live compliance dashboard with no critical gaps, and the named executive has signed a current PCI DSS compliance attestation. QSAs find compliance programmes managed by a single analyst with no executive visibility — PCI DSS v4.0 requires executive accountability and documented oversight. ROC assessments require evidence of the formal programme and executive sign-off; all SAQ types require an annual attestation.
exampleEvidence: PCI DSS compliance steering committee quarterly meeting minutes for the last 12 months, showing the executive sponsor, attendees, agenda items, and decisions made
Vanta, Drata, or Tugboat Logic GRC platform compliance dashboard screenshot showing current control status, evidence collection progress, and upcoming deadline alerts across all 12 requirement areas
Named executive's signed PCI DSS compliance attestation (AOC or internal attestation) confirming the programme is active and the requirements are being met
Compliance programme calendar for the current year showing all required activities (scans, tests, reviews, training) with assigned owners, scheduled dates, and completion status
Escalation record showing a missed compliance deadline was escalated to the executive sponsor with a documented remediation plan and revised completion date
Annual compliance review presentation delivered to the board or audit committee, showing PCI DSS scope, control status, open findings, and remediation progress

title: PCI DSS scope documented and validated
description: PCI DSS scope is documented and validated.
guidance: Document the CDE boundary annually, obtain management approval, and validate scope with a QSA if applicable.
implementationGuide: Conduct a formal scope validation exercise at least annually and after every significant change — define the CDE, all connected system components, and all systems that could impact CDE security, and document the scoping rationale. Use network discovery tools (Nmap or Qualys Asset Discovery) combined with data-discovery scans (Spirion) to confirm there are no systems touching or storing cardholder data that are not included in scope. Produce a written scope document approved by the QSA or an internal governance body. 'Done' means a scoped asset inventory is current, the data-discovery scan confirms no out-of-scope systems hold cardholder data, and the scope document has been approved within the last 12 months. QSAs find that scope creep — new systems added to the network that touch the CDE without a scope review — is one of the most common audit surprises. For ROC assessments, the QSA must independently validate scope; for SAQ assessments, the merchant must self-validate and attest.
exampleEvidence: Scope definition document listing all CDE systems, connected system components, and systems that could impact CDE security, with the scoping rationale, approved within the last 12 months
Nmap or Qualys Asset Discovery scan report for the network ranges in scope, showing all discovered hosts and their classification (CDE/connected/out-of-scope)
Spirion or Ground Labs data-discovery scan report confirming no cardholder data exists on systems classified as out-of-scope
Network diagram (approved within 30 days) showing the CDE boundary, all connected systems, and the controls enforcing each boundary, signed by the network architect

Scope change log for the last 12 months showing any systems added to or removed from the CDE scope, with the trigger event (e.g., new payment channel), the scope review date, and the updated scope document
Annual scope validation sign-off document signed by the CISO (and QSA for ROC) confirming the scope has been reviewed, validated, and is accurate as of the assessment date

title: Security awareness education ongoing
description: Security awareness education is an ongoing activity.
guidance: Deliver mandatory PCI-DSS-focused security awareness training annually; track completion and enforce consequences for non-completion.
implementationGuide: Build a security awareness training programme using KnowBe4 or Proofpoint Security Awareness Training that covers cardholder data handling, phishing recognition, physical security, password hygiene, and the acceptable use policy — all personnel with CDE access must complete this annually. Add role-specific modules for high-risk roles: developers receive secure coding training (OWASP AppSec), administrators receive privileged access security training, and customer-facing staff receive skimming and social engineering awareness. Run phishing simulation campaigns quarterly and use the results to trigger mandatory remedial training for high-risk individuals. 'Done' means the LMS shows 100% completion for the annual training module for all CDE staff, the phishing simulation click-through rate is below 5% for CDE-adjacent staff, and role-specific training is tracked separately. QSAs find that training completion records exist but the training content hasn't been updated in years — refresh content annually to reflect current threats. ROC assessments require training completion records and curriculum evidence.
exampleEvidence: KnowBe4 or Proofpoint Security Awareness Training annual completion report showing 100% of personnel with CDE access completed the current-year training module, with completion dates
Training curriculum outline showing the modules covered, including cardholder data handling, phishing, physical security, password hygiene, and AUP, with the content last-updated date
Role-specific training completion records: secure coding training for developers, privileged access training for administrators, and anti-skimming training for customer-facing staff
Quarterly phishing simulation campaign report for the most recent 4 quarters showing click-through rates, reporting rates, and trend data for CDE-adjacent staff
Remedial training assignment log showing individuals who failed phishing simulations were assigned and completed mandatory remedial training within the defined timeframe
Training content review record showing the annual training curriculum was reviewed and updated within the last 12 months to reflect current threat landscape and policy changes

title: Personnel are screened to reduce risks from insider threats
description: Personnel are screened to reduce the risks from insider threats.
guidance: Conduct background checks before granting access to the CDE; apply enhanced checks for roles with privileged CDE access.
implementationGuide: Establish a pre-employment screening policy that requires background checks for all personnel with access to CDE systems or cardholder data before access is granted — define the components of the check (identity verification, criminal record, credit history where legally permissible) and the escalation path for adverse results. Work with your HR team to ensure the background check is completed and reviewed by a named HR manager before the IT access provisioning workflow is initiated. Document the screening outcome for each individual in a confidential HR record, and rescreeen individuals in high-trust roles every two years. 'Done' means the HR system shows a background check completion record for every current CDE staff member, and no CDE access was provisioned before the check was completed and reviewed. QSAs find that contractors and temporary staff are consistently excluded from the screening programme — all personnel with physical or logical access to the CDE must be screened. ROC assessments require the screening policy and HR records; SAQ D requires the policy and a process walkthrough.
exampleEvidence: Pre-employment screening policy document defining the required check components for each CDE access role, the screening provider, the escalation procedure for adverse results, and the re-screening cadence for high-trust roles

HR background check completion log for all current employees and contractors with CDE access, showing each individual's screening date, outcome classification (pass/refer/fail), and the HR reviewer's sign-off
IT access provisioning records for the last quarter showing that CDE access was not activated until after the background check completion record was confirmed by HR
Contractor onboarding record showing the screening requirement was communicated to and completed by the contracting agency before the individual was granted CDE access
Background check provider contract or service agreement confirming the provider is accredited and the components of the check meet the policy requirements
High-trust role re-screening log showing individuals in privileged-access roles were re-screened within the required two-year window

title: Risk to information assets associated with third-party service provider relationships managed
description: Risk to information assets associated with third-party service provider (TPSP) relationships is managed.
guidance: Maintain a TPSP register, document each provider's PCI scope, and track their PCI compliance status annually.
implementationGuide: Maintain a third-party service provider (TPSP) register listing every vendor who stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data on your behalf — or who could impact the security of your CDE. For each TPSP, obtain and review their current PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AOC) or equivalent before and annually during the engagement. Define the security responsibilities between the merchant and each TPSP in a written agreement and include right-to-audit clauses. 'Done' means the TPSP register is current, every TPSP has a valid AOC on file dated within 12 months, all agreements include the security responsibility matrix, and a named owner is responsible for TPSP oversight. QSAs find TPSP registers that are incomplete — a cloud hosting provider or log management vendor with access to CDE data is a TPSP even if they don't directly touch card numbers. ROC assessments require the full register, all AOCs, and the responsibility matrices; SAQ D requires a TPSP register and current AOCs.
exampleEvidence: Third-party service provider register listing every TPSP with access to or impact on the CDE, including service type, PCI DSS scope, AOC reference number, AOC expiry date, and named internal owner
Current PCI DSS AOCs from all in-scope TPSPs, dated within 12 months, confirming the services covered match the services being provided to the merchant
Written service agreements with each TPSP including a security responsibility matrix defining which PCI DSS controls the TPSP owns and which the merchant owns
Annual TPSP AOC review records showing the security team reviewed each AOC, verified the services covered, and confirmed no material compliance gaps, with reviewer sign-off
Right-to-audit clause inclusion confirmation for all TPSP contracts, with the legal team's review sign-off
TPSP onboarding due-diligence record for the most recently engaged TPSP, showing the AOC was obtained and reviewed before the engagement began and data access was granted

title: Third-party service providers support their customers' PCI DSS compliance
description: Third-party service providers (TPSPs) support their customers' PCI DSS compliance.
guidance: Obtain written acknowledgement from all TPSPs confirming responsibility for their share of PCI DSS controls.
implementationGuide: If your organisation is a TPSP, produce and maintain a current PCI DSS AOC (or ROC if required by your customers) that accurately reflects the services in scope, and ensure it is available to customers on request. Build a standard security responsibility matrix template that clearly delineates which PCI DSS controls the TPSP owns and which the customer owns for each service offering. Establish an internal process to notify customers of any security incident or change that affects their PCI DSS compliance posture within a defined timeframe (e.g., 72 hours). 'Done' means a current AOC exists and is available to customers, the responsibility matrix is version-controlled and included in all customer contracts, and the customer notification SLA is defined and tested. QSAs find TPSPs whose AOC is outdated or covers a different scope than the services being sold — the AOC must precisely match the service being consumed. ROC assessments require the full AOC and the customer agreement templates.
exampleEvidence: Current PCI DSS AOC or ROC executive summary for the TPSP, dated within 12 months, with the QSA signature and the services/systems covered listed explicitly
Standard security responsibility matrix template used in customer contracts, showing each PCI DSS requirement and whether the TPSP or the customer is responsible for each control
Sample customer contract extract showing the security responsibility matrix, the customer's right to request the AOC, and the TPSP's obligation to notify the customer of security incidents
Internal incident notification procedure document defining the 72-hour customer notification SLA for security incidents affecting customer PCI DSS compliance, with escalation path
Customer notification test record showing a tabletop exercise or real notification was completed within the defined SLA, with the notification content and recipient list
AOC scope verification checklist confirming the services listed in the current AOC match the services being sold to PCI DSS-bound customers, reviewed by the compliance manager

title: Suspected and confirmed security incidents that could impact the CDE are responded to immediately
description: Suspected and confirmed security incidents that could impact the CDE are responded to immediately.
guidance: Maintain a CDE-specific incident response plan and test it annually with a tabletop exercise.
implementationGuide: Develop and maintain an incident response plan (IRP) specifically addressing payment card security incidents — define the trigger conditions, the incident response team (IRT) with named roles and contact details, containment steps, forensic evidence preservation procedures, and notification obligations (card brands, acquirer, regulator). Test the IRP annually via a tabletop exercise that simulates a cardholder data breach scenario, and update the plan based on lessons learned. Integrate the IRP with your SIEM (Splunk or QRadar) alerting so that the on-call analyst is paged immediately upon detection of a potential CDE compromise. 'Done' means the IRP exists and is current (reviewed within 12 months), the annual tabletop exercise has been completed, and the IRT contact list is accurate and accessible 24/7. QSAs find IRP documents that name specific individuals who have since left the organisation — maintain a current contact list and verify it quarterly. ROC assessments require the IRP document, the tabletop exercise record, and evidence of SIEM integration.
exampleEvidence: Incident response plan document specific to payment card security incidents, covering trigger conditions, IRT roles and contacts, containment steps, forensic preservation procedures, and card brand/acquirer notification obligations, reviewed within 12 months
Annual tabletop exercise record simulating a cardholder data breach, including scenario description, participants, actions taken at each stage, gaps identified, and post-exercise remediation actions
IRT contact list showing named individuals for each response role, their primary and secondary contact details, and the date of last verification (within 90 days)

Splunk or QRadar alerting configuration showing the SIEM is configured to page the on-call analyst immediately upon detection of events matching the incident trigger conditions (e.g., mass data access, exfiltration patterns)
Card brand and acquirer notification procedure showing the required notification timelines, contact details, and the template used for initial incident notification
Post-incident review record for the most recent security incident or tabletop exercise, showing root cause, timeline, containment effectiveness, lessons learned, and IRP updates made as a result
